--- Page 1 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
Shuchen Zhu * 1 Rizhen Hu * 1 Mingze Wang § 1 Mou Sun 2 Xue Wang 3 Kun Yuan † 1 Zaiwen Wen 1
Abstract
Pre-training Large Language Models requires
immense computational resources, making opti-
mizer efficiency essential. The optimization land-
scape is highly anisotropic, with loss reduction
driven predominantly by progress along flat di-
rections. While matrix-based optimizers such
as Muon and SOAP leverage fine-grained cur-
vature information to outperform AdamW, their
updates tend toward isotropy—relatively conser-
vative along flat directions yet potentially aggres-
sive along sharp ones. To address this limita-
tion, we first establish a unified Riemannian Or-
dinary Differential Equation (ODE) framework
that elucidates how common adaptive algorithms
operate synergistically: the preconditioner in-
duces a Riemannian geometry that mitigates ill-
conditioning, while momentum serves as a Rie-
mannian damping term that promotes conver-
gence. Guided by these insights, we propose
LITE, a generalized acceleration strategy that
enhances training dynamics by applying larger
Hessian damping coefficients and learning rates
along flat trajectories.
Extensive experiments
demonstrate that LITE significantly accelerates
both Muon and SOAP across diverse architectures
(Dense, MoE), parameter scales (130M–1.3B),
datasets (C4, Pile), and learning-rate schedules
(cosine, warmup-stable-decay). Theoretical anal-
ysis confirms that LITE facilitates faster conver-
gence along flat directions in anisotropic land-
scapes, providing a principled approach to effi-
cient LLM pre-training. The code is available at
https://github.com/SHUCHENZHU/LITE.
1. Introduction
Large language models (LLMs) have revolutionized artifi-
cial intelligence with exceptional capabilities, yet the pre-
*Equal contribution §Project lead 1Peking University 2Zhejiang
Lab, Zhejiang 3Meituan, Beijing. †Correspondence to: Kun Yuan
<kunyuan@pku.edu.cn>.
Preprint. February 27, 2026.
training procedure is computationally intensive due to mas-
sive model and data scales. Improving pre-training effi-
ciency is essential for continued scaling, and the optimizer
choice is crucial. Currently, AdamW (Kingma & Ba, 2014;
Loshchilov & Hutter, 2017) serves as the standard in most
LLM pre-training pipelines, favored for its simplicity and
efficiency. However, it uses coordinate-wise scaling, which
acts as a diagonal preconditioner and ignores parameter cor-
relations within the Hessian, limiting the ability to navigate
complex optimization landscapes.
To address the limitation of the diagonal preconditioning, al-
gorithms like Shampoo (Gupta et al., 2018; Shi et al., 2023)
and KFAC (Martens & Grosse, 2015) introduce matrix-
based preconditioners to capture richer geometric structures,
demonstrating superior convergence over AdamW in tra-
ditional deep learning tasks. Advancing this paradigm to
the scale of LLM pre-training, a new wave of matrix-based
optimizers, such as Muon (Jordan et al., 2024) and SOAP
(Vyas et al., 2025), has emerged, achieving significant per-
formance gains over AdamW. Notably, Muon has already
achieved validated success in industrial-scale training sce-
narios (Liu et al., 2025a; Team, 2025). These methods
signify a paradigm shift towards exploiting matrix-level
curvature information to accelerate convergence.
Despite the empirical success of these advanced optimizers,
significant limitations remain:
1. Preconditioner-induced isotropic update magnitudes.
Although adaptive methods correct descent directions by
effective preconditioners, prior studies (Staib et al., 2019;
Zhou et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2025a; Lu et al., 2025; Wang
et al., 2025b) suggest that the update magnitudes often
tend towards isotropic, i.e., of comparable scale across
different Hessian eigen-directions. This behavior is sub-
optimal in the ill-conditioned landscape, which is too
cautious along flat directions while potentially aggressive
along sharp ones.
2. Inadequate momentum mechanisms for non-convexity.
Constrained by the simple linear nature of Exponential
Moving Average (EMA), which inherently imposes an
isotropic damping effect, existing momentum schemes
inadequately exploit second-order information, leaving
them susceptible to ill-conditioned curvature. While tech-
1
arXiv:2602.22681v1  [cs.LG]  26 Feb 2026


--- Page 2 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
niques like Nesterov acceleration implicitly incorporates
Hessian-driven damping (Shi et al., 2021), the design is
largely inherited from convex optimization paradigms,
rendering them insufficient for effectively accelerating
convergence along non-convex directions.
Addressing these limitations, however, presents a funda-
mental challenge: the lack of a unified theoretical frame-
work that provides a comprehensive understanding of how
preconditioning and momentum jointly influence training
dynamics in anisotropic loss landscapes. Such a framework
remains elusive, as current analyses typically treat these
components in isolation, failing to elucidate their synergis-
tic mechanisms in non-convex optimization. Bridging this
theoretical gap is essential for designing superior optimizers
that can simultaneously rectify update magnitudes and lever-
age curvature-aware momentum. These issues motivate the
following questions:
• Can we establish a unified theoretical understanding of
the synergistic roles of the momentum and preconditioner
in adaptive optimization algorithms?
• Leveraging this foundation, how can we rectify the inade-
quate isotropic update and refine the momentum mecha-
nism to achieve superior efficiency in pre-training LLMs?
Our contributions are as follows:
• We propose a unified Riemannian ODE framework
that encapsulates prevalent adaptive optimizers for LLM
pre-training (e.g. AdamW, Lion, Muon, SOAP) as well as
their Nesterov-accelerated counterparts. This framework
elucidates the synergistic mechanism between precondi-
tioning and momentum from a continuous time manifold
optimization perspective: the preconditioner induces a
Riemannian geometry that mitigates the landscape’s ill-
conditioning, while momentum functions as a Rieman-
nian damping to foster optimization within this metric.
• Building on this framework, we propose LITE, a gener-
alized strategy for acceLerating adaptIve opTimizers in
LLM prE-training. Leveraging our ODE analysis, LITE
applies larger Hessian damping coefficients and learning
rates in the flat directions, which enhances momentum
accumulation and amplifies update magnitudes therein.
This approach is designed to accelerate training dynam-
ics along the flat directions that dominate loss reduction
within the ill-conditioned landscape, thereby enhancing
the pre-training performance.
• We conduct extensive empirical evaluations on the ac-
celeration performance of LITE across diverse LLM
pre-training settings, spanning different base optimizers
(Muon and SOAP), model architectures (LLaMA and
QwenMoE), datasets (C4 and Pile), and learning rate
schedules (cosine and warmup-stable-decay), with model
sizes ranging from 0.13B to 1.3B parameters. Our results
demonstrate that LITE-accelerated optimizers achieve
remarkable loss reductions and exhibit more favorable
scaling laws over standard baselines, notably attaining
a 2× speedup in long-horizon training (Figure 1). This
indicates the potential of LITE for superior scalability to
larger models and extended token budgets.

Figure 1. MUON-LITE exhibits superior scaling behavior across
varying token budgets (left) and model sizes (right) compared to
Muon.
• We provide a theoretical analysis of the training dynam-
ics of LITE within anisotropic landscapes, showing that
it boosts dynamics in flat directions and facilitates opti-
mization, thereby supporting our design intuition.
2. Related Work
Accelerated Optimizers for LLM pre-training.
Adap-
tive optimizers serve as the cornerstone of modern LLM
pre-training. One stream of research focuses on refining the
preconditioner: moving beyond simple element-wise scal-
ing of AdamW (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2017), methods like
(Liu et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2025a) incorporate explicit cur-
vature estimation, while matrix-based approaches (Jordan
et al., 2024; Vyas et al., 2025; Pethick et al., 2025; Liu et al.,
2025b; Lau et al., 2026) leverage richer geometric structures
to better approximate the curvature. Complementing these
geometric improvements are refinements to the momentum
mechanism, spanning multi-timescale momentum (Pagliar-
dini et al., 2025), Nesterov-type acceleration (Xie et al.,
2024; Yuan et al., 2025), and various momentum correction
schemes (Huang et al., 2025; Liang et al., 2025).
Training Dynamics and Loss Landscape in Deep Learn-
ing.
Prior studies (Ghorbani et al., 2019; Yao et al., 2020;
Zhang et al., 2024; Su, 2025) elucidate the ill-conditioned
and anisotropic nature of the deep learning loss landscape.
Specifically, the Hessian spectrum is dominated by a mas-
sive bulk of near-zero and negative eigenvalues (referred
to as flat directions), while the large positive eigenvalues
2


--- Page 3 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
are significantly greater in magnitude but sparse in number
(referred to as sharp directions), as schematically illustrated
in Figure 2 (left). Recent investigations (Song et al., 2025;
Cohen et al., 2025; Wen et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2025a)
into the training dynamics of gradient-based algorithms on
this landscape have led to the consensus that the anisotropic
landscape induces a distinct time-scale separation in the
training dynamics (Figure 2 (right)): 1) Fast dynamics:
Along sharp directions, the dynamics exhibit rapid but non-
divergent oscillations, which dictate training stability yet
contribute minimally to loss reduction. 2) Slow dynam-
ics: Along flat directions, the dynamics evolves steadily but
slowly, dominating total loss reduction.
Leveraging these insights, our LITE approach is designed to
accelerate training by boosting the slow training dynamics
along flat directions (see schematic in Figure 2 (right)).
More related works are deferred to Appendix A.
-1
0
1
2
3
4
5
Eigenvalue (×10
4)
10
2
10
1
100
Normalized Frequency
Hessian Eigenvalues Distribution
Flat
Sharp
progress slowly along flat directions
oscillate along sharp directions
Figure 2. (left) Hessian eigenvalue distribution of an up proj
FFN block in a toy LLaMA model; (right) Schematic illustration of
the time-scale separation property and the acceleration mechanism
of the LITE approach.
3. Preliminary
Notations.
Let vk denote the k-th component of a vec-
tor v. For a positive semi-definite matrix F, we define
the induced inner product and norm as ⟨u, v⟩F = u⊤Fv
and ∥u∥F
=
√
u⊤Fu respectively, omitting the sub-
script when F
= I.
Given a matrix-valued function
F : Rp →Rp×p, its directional derivative is written as
∇F(w)[v] = limϵ→0(F(w + ϵv) −F(w))/ϵ. We distin-
guish between Riemannian operators (grad, Hess) and their
Euclidean counterparts (∇, ∇2). The symbol ⊙represents
the element-wise product, and ⊗denotes the Kronecker
product. A subscript or superscript F is occasionally at-
tached to geometric operators (e.g. ∇F ) to emphasize they
are induced by the Riemannian metric F.
3.1. Manifold Optimization
Manifold optimization deals with variables constrained to a
Riemannian manifold. In this work, we consider a special
case: the variable (parameter) space Rp not as a standard Eu-
clidean space, but as a Riemannian manifold M = (Rp, F)
equipped with metric F(w) ∈Rp×p at each w ∈Rp. This
geometric perspective allows us to analyze the optimization
landscape through the lens of local curvature defined by F.
Detailed discussions are provided in Appendix F.
Tangent and Cotangent Spaces.
At any point w ∈M,
the tangent space TwM ∼= Rp represents the vector space
of all possible directional velocities (or parameter perturba-
tions). The Riemannian metric induces a norm ∥· ∥F (w) on
the tangent space. The cotangent space T ∗
wM is defined as
the dual space to TwM, which is equipped with the norm
∥· ∥F (w)−1 and is also isomorphism to Rp. The metric
induces linear isomorphisms F(w)−1 : T ∗
wM →TwM
and F(w) : TwM →T ∗
wM. While the Euclidean gradient
∇f(w) naturally resides in T ∗
wM, the Riemannian gradi-
ent is obtained by mapping it back to the tangent space via
grad f(w) = F(w)−1∇f(w). It gives the steepest descent
steps in the local geometry:
−F(w)−1∇f(w) = lim
ϵ→0 argmin
∥h∥F (w)≤ϵ
f(w + h) −f(w)
ϵ
.
Levi-Civita Connection.
Levi-Civita connection ∇(·)(·)
provides a rigorous tool for differentiating vector fields on
Riemannian manifolds, generalizing the concept of direc-
tional derivatives from Euclidean space to curved geome-
tries. For vector fields u, v, ∇uv yields a vector field that
quantifies how v twists or deviates from a parallel field
as it moves along u. Levi-Civita connection is essential
for defining the Riemannian Hessian, which is given by
Hessf(w)[u] = ∇u grad f(w) for any vector field u.
3.2. ODE Perspective of Momentum-based Algorithms
Continuous-time modeling via ODEs constitutes a rigorous
and principled paradigm for analyzing momentum-based
algorithms, offering deep theoretical insights and clear in-
tuitions into their underlying dynamics. (Su et al., 2016)
proposed a second-order inertial system that characterizes
the continuous-time limit to Nesterov accelerated gradient
(momentum) method. Further, (Shi et al., 2021; Attouch
et al., 2022) introduced an inertial system with Hessian
damping (ISHD) that can depict the momentum-based algo-
rithms in higher resolution, which takes the form
¨wt + αt ˙wt + βt∇2f(wt) ˙wt + γt∇f(wt) = 0,
(1)
where αt, βt, γt ≥0 denote the coefficients for momentum
decay, Hessian damping, and the gradient driving force,
respectively.
Discretizing this system recovers various
momentum methods: specifically, setting βt = 0 yields
Heavy Ball momentum, while βt > 0 corresponds to
Nesterov-type momentum. Crucially, the Hessian damping
term ∇2f(wt) ˙wt and its discretized counterpart ∇f(wk) −
∇f(wk−1) serves to mitigate oscillations, thereby enhanc-
ing the stability of standard Heavy Ball dynamics. Further
details on discretization, equivalent formulations, and the
role of Hessian damping are provided in Section E.
3


--- Page 4 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
4. A Unified Riemannian ODE Framework for
Understanding Adaptive Algorithms
Adaptive optimizers synergize momentum with precondi-
tioners, leveraging momentum-induced acceleration while
simultaneously adapting step sizes anisotropically for effi-
cient convergence. In this section, we interpret the mechan-
ics of these algorithms by modeling them as inertial systems
(ODEs) with Hessian damping on a Riemannian manifold
(RISHD) equipped with preconditioner-induced metrics.
4.1. A Unified Discrete Formulation
Formally, the update rules of most adaptive optimizers can
be encapsulated within a single unified formulation:
(
mk = (1 −α)mk−1 + ∇f(wk),
wk+1 = wk −ηkF(wk)−1(mk + β∇f(wk)),
(2)
where wk denotes the parameters, mk is the momentum
1, F(wk)−1 serves as the preconditioner, and the term
β∇f(wk) yields a Nesterov-type momentum.
In the context of neural network training, the design of
the preconditioner F −1 is typically guided by: 1) Block-
Diagonal Structure: To manage computational complexity,
F is generally treated as a block-diagonal matrix, where
each block corresponds to the parameter tensor of a specific
layer; 2) EMA Estimation: Instead of computing exact curva-
ture, EMA is commonly used to estimate F(w) to suppress
stochastic noise from mini-batch gradients and stabilize the
estimation of the local curvature; 3) Fisher-type Approxi-
mation: Fundamentally, most adaptive algorithms aim to
approximate the Fisher-type metric ˆF(w) = (E[gg⊤])
1
2 ,
where g is the stochastic gradient of a specific block.
By varying the structure of F and the momentum coeffi-
cient β, the formulation (2) subsumes a broad family of
adaptive methods, including N-AdamW, Muon, and Soap.
See Table 1 and Appendix I for further details.
Optimizers using Hessian Damping
Several recent op-
timizers, including Lion, MARS (Yuan et al., 2025), and
Muon, incorporate Nesterov-style momentum to surpass the
performance of vanilla Heavy Ball momentum. The key
step is preconditioning mk + βgk as in (2) rather than the
standard momentum mk. As discussed in Appendix E, the
βgk term induces an implicit Hessian damping effect by
∇f(wk) −∇f(wk−1) ≈∇2f(wk)(wk −wk−1), crucial
for reducing oscillations on ill-conditioned landscapes.
4.2. The Riemannian ODE Framework
Continuous-time Viewpoint.
Now we interpret the gen-
eralized form (2) as a approximated discretization of a Rie-
1The missing coefficient α of ∇f can be absorbed in ηk.
mannian ODE. Leveraging Levi-Civita connection ∇F
(·)(·)
to generalize the Euclidean derivatives to the covariant
derivatives, we can extend the Euclidean ISHD (1) to the
Riemannian ISHD (RISHD) on (M, F):
∇F
˙wt ˙wt+αt ˙wt+βt HessF (wt) ˙wt+γt gradF f(wt)=0. (3)
where HessF and gradF denote F-induced Riemannian
Hessian and gradient respectively.
First-order Tangent-Cotangent Formulation.
We can
introduce the momentum variable mt tracking the velocity
˙wt to decouple (3) into a first order system that evolves in
the tangent space (T) and cotangent space (C) respectively:
Proposition 4.1 (Adapted from Proposition G.2). If αt =
α −˙ηt/ηt, βt = βηt, γt = ηt(αβ + 1), (3) is equivalent to
(
˙wt = −ηtF(wt)−1(mt + β∇f(wt)),
˙mt = −αmt + ∇f(wt) + Rt,
(T)
(C)
where the Riemannian correction term Rt is defined in (53).
The term Rt mainly depends on ηt and ∇F(wt). It is typ-
ically negligible compared to ∇f(wt) and mt due to the
small step size ηt and the stable preconditioner (implying a
small ∇F(wt)). Thus we can ignore Rt for brevity.
The decoupled formulation in (T) and (C) elucidate the na-
ture of RISHD in (3): Momentum evolves in the cotangent
space, while the preconditioner F −1 maps it to the tangent
space to drive the parameter update. This form also naturally
gives the specific form in which modern adaptive methods
incorporate momentum: the preconditioner is applied to the
momentum accumulated from raw gradients, rather than ac-
cumulating preconditioned gradients by EMA. Furthermore,
this Riemannian ODE framework is readily extensible to
higher-order cases, enabling the characterization of complex
momentum schemes such as the multi-timescale momentum
in AdEMAMix (Pagliardini et al., 2025) (Appendix E.3).
Joint effect of the preconditioner and momentum.
The
continuous-time ODE framework (3) is instrumental and
intuitive in elucidating the joint mechanism of precondi-
tioning and momentum, which remains opaque in discrete
formulations. Assuming F(w) is a slowly varying Hes-
sian approximation, the induced Riemannian metric effec-
tively mitigates ill-conditioning, as it yields a Riemannian
Hessian HessF f(w) ≈F −1(w)∇2f(w) which exhibits a
much better condition number than the Euclidean Hessian
∇2f(w). Leveraging this improved geometry, the damp-
ing term −(αt + βt Hess f(w)) ˙wt, which encapsulates the
momentum effect in the continuous dynamics, then acts to
further accelerate convergence.
Connection with the Discrete Formulation.
Compared
to the original second-order system (3), the equivalent first-
order coupled system offers a more direct path for numerical

variant (N-AdamW). See Appendix I for further discussions and details.

implementation. Applying a semi-implicit Euler discretiza-
tion scheme to (T) and (C) with step size h = 1 and Rt ≈0
yields the discrete formulation (2).
5. The LITE Approach
In this section, we introduce LITE, an acceleration strategy
grounded in the characteristics of the loss landscape and
the theoretical insights regarding the synergistic role of
momentum and preconditioning established in Section 4.2.
As discussed in Section 2, the trajectory along sharp di-
rections exhibits significant fluctuations, and the efficiency
of traversal along flat directions becomes the critical de-
terminant of the final loss. Motivated by this, our strategy
aims to selectively accelerate optimization along flat di-
rections while preserving stability in sharp directions.
To implement this, we adopt a decoupled tuning scheme:
we anchor the hyper-parameters governing the sharp direc-
tions in (2) to maintain stability, while adaptively adjusting
those corresponding to the flat directions. In the follow-
ing discussion, we derive a heuristic adjustment scheme for
the flat-direction hyper-parameters from the perspective of
continuous-time dynamics in Section 4.
5.1. Acceleration Methods from the ODE Perspective
We begin by analyzing the role of momentum through the
lens of the continuous-time limit in (1), focusing initially on
the Euclidean case of (3) for clarity. Intuitively, the system
models the dynamics of a particle moving within a potential
field defined by f. The particle at wt is subject to the com-
bined effects of a driving force −γt∇f(wt) and a curvature-
adaptive damping (drag force) −(αtI + βt∇2f(wt)) ˙wt op-
posing its velocity ˙wt, with its acceleration ¨wt governed by
Newton’s Second Law. Along flat directions, we can en-
hance the accumulation of velocity by increasing the driving
force coefficient γt while minimizing the effective damping
(i.e. reducing the eigenvalues of αtI + βt∇2f(wt)). Since
flat directions are often characterized by non-convexity, our
strategy is increasing γt and βt in the flat directions. 2
2Regarding damping, we do not tune αt for the sake of simplic-
ity. While αt offers a similar mechanism for reducing damping,
Algorithm 1 LITE Strategy
1: Input: w0, m0, {ηk}, α, χ ≥1, β2 ≥β1 ≥0.
2: for k = 0 to K do
3:
Update momentum mk = (1 −α)mk−1 + ∇f(wk).
4:
Estimate projection Qk onto the flat direction.
5:
Estimate the update direction
uk =(I −Qk)F(wk)−1(mk + β1∇f(wk))
+ χQkF(wk)−1(mk + β2∇f(wk)).
6:
Update parameters wk+1 = wk −ηkuk.
7: end for
The above discussion, framed in terms of damping and
motion in Euclidean space, can be naturally extended to
Riemannian manifolds (curved spaces). The corresponding
physical background shifts from Newtonian mechanics to
Lagrangian mechanics (Arnold, 1989), yet the same con-
clusions hold. For sharp directions, the coefficients remain
unchanged to maintain stability conditions. Given the re-
lationships γt = (1 + αβ)ηt and βt = βηt in (3), we can
amplify γt and βt by increasing β and ηt. Finally, assuming
well-aligned top eigenspaces between F and the Hessian,
the accelerated dynamics is given by:





˙wt = −ηtF(wt)−1Pt(mt + β1∇f(wt))
−χηtF(wt)−1Qt(mt + β2∇f(wt)),
˙mt = −αmt + ∇f(wt),
(4)
where Pt is the projection to the sharp subspace, Qt =
I −Pt, and χ ≥1, β2 ≥β1 ≥0 are hyper-parameters. Dis-
cretizing (4) like (2) yields the accelerated algorithms frame-
work LITE (Algorithm 1), where we simply notations for
brevity. This formulation naturally yields amplified update
magnitudes along flat directions, facilitating faster traversal
through these slow-progressing regimes. An illustrative the-
oretical analysis of the LITE acceleration mechanism on a
quadratic function is provided in Appendix H.
βt is preferred as it provides curvature adaptivity.

Figure 3. Coverage of the top eigenspaces of row (column) Hes-
sians by those of G⊤G (GG⊤) for the up proj block of an FFN
layer. A higher score indicates a greater degree of containment.
5.2. Practical Implementation of LITE
In this section, we present practical execution strategies
for implementing LITE (Algorithm 1) in LLM-pretraining.
Our approach specifically targets the anisotropic landscape
inherent to each parameter block. The core objective is to
efficiently identify the flat (or sharp) directions within each
parameter block.
Efficiently approximating the flat directions
Prior re-
search (Martens, 2020; Morwani et al., 2025) demonstrates
that the Fisher matrix and Shampoo-like preconditioners
effectively approximates the Gauss-Newton component, the
dominant term in the Hessian. Since Muon and SOAP adopt
Shampoo-like methods to approximate the square root of the
Fisher matrix via Kronecker factorization, we hypothesize
that the top eigenspaces of their preconditioners and the
Hessian are highly aligned.
To verify this hypothesis, we conducted experiments on a toy
LLaMA-2 model (see experimental details in Appendix C.1).
Figure 2 (left) confirms that the landscape within each block
is ill-conditioned, with sharp directions accounting for only
a small proportion of the spectrum. Besides, Figure 3 in-
dicate that for a small dimension ds = 4 (4/32 and 4/16),
the top-ds eigenspaces of the Hessian (for rows/columns)
can be effectively covered by a subspace of G⊤G (GG⊤)
of dimension only slightly larger than ds, where G denotes
the stochastic gradient matrix. Consequently, by taking the
orthogonal complement, we can obtain a reliable approxi-
mation of the flat directions. Since preconditioners of Muon
and SOAP are constructed from G⊤G (GG⊤), we propose
the following acceleration schemes tailored for them.
Accelerating Muon (MUON-LITE)
At iteration k, let
Gk ∈Rm×n (m ≥n) represent the stochastic gradient for
a given parameter block. The update process begins with
the momentum accumulation Mk = (1 −α)Mk−1 + Gk,
followed by the Nesterov correction f
Mk = Mk + Gk/(1 −
α) 3. Recognizing that f
M ⊤
k f
Mk acts as a robust proxy for the
expected covariance E[G⊤
k Gk] (Appendix I.1), we utilize
3We adopt the Nesterov formulation consistent with (Jordan
et al., 2024).
it to characterize the landscape geometry. Specifically, we
construct the projection matrix Pk ∈Rn×n onto the top-ds
eigenspace of f
M ⊤
k f
Mk, where ds specifies the dimension of
the sharp subspace. A numerically efficient, optimizer state-
free method for estimating Pk is detailed in Appendix B.1.
Consequently, the update direction is formulated as:
Uk =f
Mk(f
M ⊤
k f
Mk)−1
2 (Pk + χ(In −Pk))
+ Gk(G⊤
k Gk)−1
2 (β1Pk + χβ2(In −Pk)),
(5)
where β2 ≥β1 are the Hessian damping coefficients, and
χ ≥1 denotes the amplification ratio of the learning rate
in flat directions relative to sharp ones. Setting χ = 1 and
β1,2 = 0 recovers the original Muon baseline. Here we use
the precondition (G⊤
k Gk)−1
2 instead of (f
M ⊤
k f
Mk)−1
2 to the
gradient term, since it has superior numerical stability and
efficiency of applying NS iterations, and G⊤
k Gk itself serves
as a valid instantaneous estimator of E[G⊤
k Gk].
Accelerating SOAP (SOAP-LITE)
We retain the nota-
tion established above. As detailed in Table 1, the SOAP
preconditioner explicitly provides the eigenvalues (V ) and
eigenvectors (Qr ⊗Ql) of the curvature estimation. Con-
sequently, we can identify the sharp directions directly in
the (Qr ⊗Ql)-rotated eigenspace by selecting the top-ds
(ds ≤mn) elements of V . Let Pk ∈Rm×n denote the
mask matrix indicating the sharp directions at iteration k,
and let Qk = 1m1⊤
n −Pk represent the flat directions. The
update direction for accelerating SOAP is given by:
Uk =Ql

V
−1
2
k
⊙(β1Pk + χβ2Qk) ⊙(Q⊤
l GkQr)

Q⊤
r
+ Ql

V
−1
2
k
⊙(Pk + χQk) ⊙(Q⊤
l MkQr)

Q⊤
r ,
where, similar to MUON-LITE, the SOAP baseline is re-
covered when χ = 1 and β1,2 = 0. In practice, to enhance
training stability, we implement Pk using a soft transition
scheme (smoothly changing from 0 to 1) rather than a hard
binary cutoff. See Appendix B.2 for implementation details.
6. Experiments
We evaluate LITE in accelerating Muon and SOAP on a
wide range of LLM pre-training scenarios. Experimental
details are provided in Appendix B and C.
6.1. Results on Dense Models
Main Results.
Figures 4 and 5 compare the performance
of MUON-LITE and SOAP-LITE against vanilla Muon
and SOAP across a range of LLaMA model sizes on both
the C4 and Pile datasets. The values of {χ, β1,2} in LITE
were determined via a search on the 0.25B model experi-
ments and were then used uniformly for other experiments.
6

Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement

LLaMA (1.3B) on Pile

Muon-LITE
Figure 4. Performance comparison of Muon and MUON-LITE on LLM pre-training tasks. We evaluate LLaMA2 models across various
sizes and datasets using a cos learning rate schedule. The suffixes L and H denote two ablation variants of LITE: in flat directions,
L increases only the learning rate ratio χ ≥1, while H increases only the Hessian damping coefficient β2. The token batch size is
approximately 2M for the 1.3B model, and 1M for others.
Across all experimental settings, MUON-LITE and SOAP-
LITE consistently achieve lower terminal losses than their
respective well-tuned baselines. Besides, LITE outperforms
both LITE-L and LITE-H, validating the effectiveness of
simultaneously increasing χ and β2. To further assess scala-
bility, we visualize the scaling laws of MUON-LITE versus
Muon in Figure 1 (right). The performance gains yielded
by MUON-LITE remain consistent across a wide range of
model scales, indicating its potential scalability. Further
downstream analyses are provided in Appendix D.
We subsequently investigate the acceleration efficacy of
LITE over extended training horizons, increasing the train-
ing token budget from 40× (10k iterations) the parameter
count to 100× (25k) and 200× (50k). Figure 1 (left) indi-
cates that the loss reduction gains yielded by MUON-LITE
persist across these extended regimes. Notably, at the 100×
setting, MUON-LITE achieves an approximate 2× speedup
over Muon. This demonstrates the sustained scalability of
MUON-LITE with respect to the number of training tokens.

LLaMA (0.25B) on Pile

SOAP-LITE
Figure 5. Performance comparison of SOAP and SOAP-LITE on
LLM pre-training tasks. Same experimental setup and notations
are employed as in Figure 4.
Ablation Studies.
We demonstrate that the acceleration
strategy for flat directions is detrimental when applied to
sharp directions. Under the experimental setting for the
0.25B model in Figure 4, we tested applying uniform damp-
ing coefficients β1,2 = 0.5 and β1,2 = 1.0. Despite these
values being smaller than the coefficient used for flat direc-
tions in MUON-LITE-H (β2 = 2.0), they yielded terminal
losses of 2.113 and 2.129 respectively, both even inferior to
the vanilla Muon baseline (2.110). The results validate our
design choice of maintaining hyper-parameters along sharp
directions when selectively accelerating the flat ones.
6.2. Results on MoE Models
Mixture-of-experts (MoE) architectures have emerged as a
critical paradigm for modern LLMs. By selectively activat-
ing a subset of experts for each token, MoE models achieve
superior scaling efficiency over their dense counterparts.
0
5k
10k
15k
20k
num of iterations
1.9
2.0
validation loss
0.025
QwenMoE (1B) on Pile
Muon
Muon-LITE
Figure 6. MUON-LITE outper-
forms Muon in QwenMoE pre-
training tasks.
We
evaluate
MUON-
LITE
against
the
Muon
baseline
on
a
1B-parameter QwenMoE
model, utilizing a wsd
learning
rate
schedule.
The results demonstrate
that MUON-LITE con-
sistently
outperforms
Muon
throughout
the
entire training trajectory,
ultimately
achieving
a
significantly
lower
terminal loss (Figure 6).
Furthermore, we observe that the performance gap between
MUON-LITE and Muon progressively widens as iterations
proceed during the stable phase. This trend suggests that
the efficiency gains offered by MUON-LITE are likely to
persist or even amplify at larger data scales.
7. Theoretical Analysis to Training Dynamics
In this section, we provide a theoretical characterization of
the acceleration mechanism of LITE. We adopt and extend
the River-Valley landscape framework recently proposed
by (Wen et al., 2025), which models the pre-training loss
landscape as a deep valley with a flat river at its bottom.
7


--- Page 8 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
We generalize this framework to high-dimensional settings,
and analyze the dynamics (4) corresponding to LITE (Algo-
rithm 1). Our analysis demonstrates that LITE effectively
accelerates the training dynamics along the River, which
dictates the terminal loss. In the following, we introduce the
assumptions required for our theoretical derivation.
Assumption 7.1 (River Structure). Fix an integer ds ∈Z+
and an open set U ⊂Rp.
Let Ps(w) denote the Eu-
clidean projection onto the top ds eigenspace of the Hessian
∇2f(w). We assume that Ps(w) varies smoothly with re-
spect to w, and that the set (referred to as River)
R = {w ∈U : Ps(w)∇f(w) = 0}
(6)
constitutes a (p −ds)-dimensional manifold. Furthermore,
we assume that within the sharp subspace Range(Ps(w)),
the Hessian ∇2f(w) and the preconditioner F(w) share a
common eigen-basis. Finally, the ODE
d
dtψt(w) = −Ps(ψt(w))F −1(ψt(w))∇f(ψt(w)),
(7)
initialized at any ψ0(w) = w ∈U is assumed to remain and
converge within U as t →∞.
Under Assumption 7.1, we define the “projection” map to
River Φ : U →R via the limit of the flow: Φ(w) :=
limt→∞ψt(w). This ensures Φ(w) ∈R since the station-
arity of the flow implies Ps∇f = 0.
Assumption 7.2 (Regularity, Smoothness and Conserva-
tion). Given the domain U in Assumption 7.1, we assume:
1. f is analytic in U.
Let (·)|s denote the restriction
of an operator to the sharp subspace Range(Ps(w)).
For all w ∈U, the eigenvalues of ∇2f(w), F(w),
F(w)|s, (F −1
2 (w)∇2f(w)F −1
2 (w))|s lie in [−L, L],
[λF , ρ], [λFs, ρ] and [λHF ,s, LHF ,s], respectively.
2. There exist constants δ, δF , ε ≥0 with max{δ, δF } ≤ε,
such that for any w ∈U, ∥∇f(w)∥F (w)−1
≤G,
∥∇Ps(w)∥op ≤δ/G and ∥∇F(w)∥op ≤δF /G. See
(75) for detailed definition of the operator norm ∥· ∥op.
3. There exists a time horizon Tmax > 0, dependent on
the hyper-parameters (α, β1, {ηt}t≥0, χ, β2), such that
for any initialization w0 ∈U with initial momentum
m0 = 0, the trajectory wt generated by the dynamics (4)
remains strictly within U for all t ∈[0, Tmax].
Theorem 7.3. Suppose Assumptions 7.1 and 7.2 hold. De-
fine the decay rate ιt = min{α/2, λHF,sβ1ηt} and the for-
getting kernel K(t) = exp

−
R t
0 ιsds

. Assuming ˙ηt ≤0,
there exists a constant ε0 > 0 (dependent on f and the
hyper-parameters in (4)) such that for all ε ≤ε0, the trajec-
tory wt governed by (4) satisfies the attraction bound:
∥wt −Φ(wt)∥2
2 ≲K(t) + ε,
(8)
and the “projected” trajectory zt = Φ(wt) follows the
coupled dynamics:
(
˙zt =−ηt
␀χPR(zt)F(zt)−1(st+β2∇f(zt))+ϵz,t

,
˙st =−αst+∇f(zt),
(9)
with s0 = 0, where ϵz,t denotes a perturbation term.
Finally, defining bt = ∥PR(zt)F(zt)−1∇f(zt)∥2
F (zt) +
∥PR(zt)F(zt)−1st∥2
F (zt), the perturbation error satisfies:
∥ϵz,t∥2
F (zt) ≲K(t) + ε2bt + ε
Z t
0
K(τ −t)bτdτ.
(10)
The proof is in Lemmas J.6 and J.11. We treat ε as a small
constant to enforce a slowly varying R. Inspired by (At-
touch et al., 2022), we can take ηt ≡η, α = 2λ1/2
FH,sη,
β1 = λ−1/2
FH,s to achieve a fast decay K(t) = exp(−ηλ1/2
FH,st)
(i.e. fast convergence on sharp directions). In this case, The-
orem 7.3 indicates that the dynamics (4) first converge to the
O(ε1/2) neighborhood of R after a Θ(η−1λ−1/2
FH,s log(ε−1))
interval, then faithfully track the decoupled acceleration
dynamics on R closely, where the tracking error exhibits a
fading memory property, driven predominantly by the im-
mediate history of the underlying dynamics. Next we clarify
how χ and β2 influence the convergence of the decoupled
dynamics in R, where the detailed proof is in Lemma J.12.
Theorem 7.4. Suppose Assumptions 7.1, 7.2 hold and ˙ηt ≤
0. Let ∆f = f(z0) −inf f. For the unperturbed dynamics
in (9) with ϵz,t ≡0, there exists a constant ε1 (depending
on f and hyper-parameters in (4)), such that for any ε ≤ε1
and any T ≤Tmax, the trajectory zt satisfies
Z T
0
ηt∥PR(zt)F(zt)−1∇f(zt)∥2
F (zt)dt ≤2∆f
χβ2
,
Z T
0
ηt∥st∥2
F (zt)dt ≤2∆f
χα .
Theorem 7.4 demonstrates that increasing both χ ≥1 and
β2 yields a tighter integral bound for the Riemannian gradi-
ent on R (i.e. PR(zt)F(zt)−1∇f(zt)) and the momentum
st. This theoretical result validates the effectiveness of the
strategy employed in LITE.
8. Conclusion
We propose LITE, a generalized strategy to accelerate LLM
pre-training by enhancing training dynamics along flat di-
rections. Experimentally, LITE significantly enhances state-
of-the-art optimizers such as Muon and SOAP. Our results
indicate that LITE consistently achieves lower terminal loss
and exhibits superior scaling behavior compared to corre-
sponding baselines across various LLM pre-training tasks.
For future work, we plan to explore adaptive mechanisms
for dynamic hyper-parameter tuning within LITE, and ex-
tending its integration with other emerging optimizers.
8


--- Page 9 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
Impact Statement
This paper presents work whose goal is to advance the field
of Machine Learning, with a focus on understanding and
improving the pre-training of LLMs. There are many poten-
tial societal consequences of our work, none which we feel
must be specifically highlighted here.

hessian: Neural networks through the lens of the hessian.
In 2020 IEEE international conference on big data (Big
data), pp. 581–590. IEEE, 2020.
Yuan, H., Liu, Y., Wu, S., Xun, Z., and Gu, Q. MARS:
Unleashing the power of variance reduction for training
large models. In Forty-second International Conference
on Machine Learning, 2025.
Zhang, Y., Chen, C., Ding, T., Li, Z., Sun, R., and Luo,
Z.-Q. Why transformers need adam: A hessian perspec-
tive. In The Thirty-eighth Annual Conference on Neural
Information Processing Systems, 2024.
Zhou, P., Feng, J., Ma, C., Xiong, C., Hoi, S. C. H., and E, W.
Towards theoretically understanding why sgd generalizes
better than adam in deep learning. In Advances in Neural
Information Processing Systems, volume 33, pp. 21285–
21296, 2020.
Zhou, W., Wang, B., Chen, W., and Cheng, X. Bsfa: Lever-
aging the subspace dichotomy to accelerate neural net-
work training, 2025. URL https://arxiv.org/
abs/2510.25244.

12


--- Page 13 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
A. More Related Works
Comparison with methods enhancing dynamics along flat directions.
Recent strategies accelerate LLM pre-training
by assigning larger learning rates to flat components. (Wang et al., 2024) adjust learning rates based on an element-wise
diagonal Fisher approximation, while (Wang et al., 2025a) apply block-wise learning rates to blocks identified as flat via
averaged sharpness. (Zhou et al., 2025) further employs a matrix-level precision subspace detected via a lazy PCA-based
estimator. Compared to these methods, LITE identifies flat subspaces with matrix-level precision using highly efficient
numerical techniques that introduce no extra optimizer states. Crucially, prior studies focus exclusively on learning rate
adjustment, a strategy LITE subsumes by simply setting χ ≥1. In contrast, we also address the dynamics of momentum
in anisotropic landscapes. We elucidate how momentum and preconditioners jointly influence training dynamics and
enhance momentum evolution in flat directions by explicitly increasing Hessian damping.
Comparison with algorithmic frameworks for adaptive optimizers.
Recent works (Pethick et al., 2025; Bernstein &
Newhouse, 2024; Xie et al., 2026) interpret the update step of certain adaptive optimizers as the solution to a constrained
optimization problem governed by operator norms:
U ∗= argmin
∥U∥op≤1
⟨U, M⟩,
(11)
where M denotes the momentum at the current iterate, ⟨·, ·⟩is the Frobenius inner product, and ∥· ∥op represents a specific
operator norm. This formulation formally unifies methods like Muon and Lion, offering insights into their stability (e.g.,
by controlling the magnitude of activations during propagation). However, a limitation is that the induced geometry is
typically independent of the local curvature at the current iterate, making it difficult to encompass methods that decouple
momentum and precondition updates like AdamW and SOAP, which rely on iterates-dependent preconditions. Furthermore,
this perspective fails to justify the algorithmic necessity of accumulating momentum before preconditioning. In contrast,
our proposed Riemannian ODE framework unifies a broader class of adaptive optimizers. It elucidates the joint role of
momentum and preconditioners in shaping training dynamics and extends naturally to complex momentum schemes (see
Appendix E.3).
B. Algorithm Details
B.1. Details of MUON-LITE
For Muon and MUON-LITE, we adopt the Newton-Schulz (NS) iteration termed Polar Express in (Amsel et al., 2025), and
set Newton–Schulz iterations to 6. This approach yields faster convergence to the theoretical optimum than the method in
(Jordan et al., 2024).
For the embedding, norm, output blocks, we use the same methods as in SOAP-LITE with Ql = I, Qr = I to
estimate the projection Pk to sharp directions. See more details in Section B.2. Next we introduce how to Pk for other
blocks using MUON-LITE.
Estimating Pk via Efficient Composite Newton-Schulz.
To efficiently estimate the sharp subspace projection Pk at itera-
tion k, we employ a lightweight composite Newton-Schulz (NS) scheme. The process involves two main steps: constructing
a filtering operator and dynamically adjusting the threshold. For any matrix A with Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)
A = UΣV ⊤, We define NS(A) = A(A⊤A)−1
2 = UV ⊤, where the inverse denotes the Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse.
1. Construction of the Filtering Operator. We first define a dynamic threshold τk = lk∥f
Mk∥F , where lk is a scaling
factor. This threshold τk serves to delineate the sharp subspace: specifically, we select the eigenspace of f
M ⊤
k f
Mk associated
with eigenvalues larger than τk. The adaptive update mechanism for lk (and thus τk) will be detailed subsequently. We then
compute the filtering operator Tk ∈Rm×n as:
Tk = 1
2 NS(f
Mk) + 1
2 NS
 f
Mk
τk
−NS(f
Mk)
!
.
(12)
The projection matrix onto the sharp subspace is subsequently obtained by Pk = T ⊤
k Tk, the rationale for which is detailed
in the subsequent parts ((14) and (15)). It is worth noting that this method for estimating Pk necessitates relatively high
13


--- Page 14 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
precision from the NS function. Consequently, we employ the Polar Express method (Amsel et al., 2025) rather than the
vanilla NS iterations used in (Jordan et al., 2024).
2. Dynamic Sharp Subspace Dimension Adjustment. To ensure the rank of the projection Pk approximates the target
dimension ds, we update the scaling factor lk based on the effective rank (measured by ∥Pk∥2
F ):
lk+1 =
(
1.05 · lk,
if ∥Pk∥F ≥√ds,
0.95 · lk,
if ∥Pk∥F < √ds.
(13)
This feedback loop effectively maintains τk near the ds-th largest singular value of f
Mk.
Theoretical Intuition. To understand why Tk acts as a subspace filter, consider the SVD of f
Mk = UΣV ⊤. Let the singular
values be ordered as σ1 ≥· · · ≥σj > τk > σj+1 ≥· · · ≥0. Since the Newton-Schulz iteration converges to the matrix
sign function, we have NS(f
Mk) = UV ⊤. Consequently, the input to the second NS term becomes:
f
Mk
τk
−NS(f
Mk) = U diag
σ1
τk
−1, . . . , σr
τk
−1

V ⊤.
(14)
Applying NS(·) to (14) maps positive entries (where σi > τk) to +1 and negative entries (where σi < τk) to −1. Therefore,
we have
Tk = 1
2 NS(f
Mk) + 1
2 NS
 f
Mk
τk
−NS(f
Mk)
!
= U diag(1, . . . , 1, 0, . . . , 0)V ⊤.
(15)
Computational Efficiency of MUON-LITE. Overall, MUON-LITE requires only two additional NS functions per step
compared to vanilla Muon, which can be executed in parallel. Since NS iterations constitute a nearly negligible fraction of
the computational cost in large-batch pre-training (dominated by gradient computation), this slight overhead is virtually
imperceptible in practice. For example, training a LLaMA-1.3B model on the Pile dataset (global batch size 8192, micro
batch size 16, sequence length 1024, on 8× A800-80GB GPUs) yields a throughput of 100.4k tokens/s for MUON-LITE
versus 101.5k tokens/s for Muon. This reflects a throughput drop of only ≈1%. Furthermore, efficiency can be enhanced
via system-level optimizations, such as kernel fusion for the NS iterations.
B.2. Details of SOAP-LITE
In SOAP-LITE, we employ a dynamic thresholding strategy generating a smoothed projection onto the sharp directions to
enhance training stability. To avoid the computational latency and memory overhead associated with exact top k operations
in PyTorch, especially for large matrices, we approximate the top-k thresholds via adaptive scalar variables, similar to the
approach in MUON-LITE. We define ds as the target dimension of the sharp subspace, and dsmooth as the dimension of the
transitional subspace bridging the sharp and flat directions. We maintain two dynamic scalars, ls
k (controlling the sharp
boundary) and lsmooth
k
(controlling the smooth boundary), initialized as ls
0 = 1.0 and lsmooth
0
= 0.5.
At the k-th iteration, we estimate the thresholds for the top-ds and top-(ds + dsmooth) elements based on the mean magnitude
of the tensor Vk: τ s
k = ls
k mean(Vk) and τ smooth
k
= lsmooth
k
mean(Vk). The smoothed projection matrix Pk ∈Rm×n is then
constructed element-wise as:
(Pk)i,j =











1,
if (Vk)i,j ≥τ s
k,
(Vk)i,j −τ smooth
k
τ s
k −τ smooth
k
,
if τ smooth
k
≤(Vk)i,j < τ s
k,
0,
if (Vk)i,j ≤τ smooth
k
.
(16)
Then we dynamically adjust the coefficients ls
k and lsmooth
k
based on the current sparsity levels:
ls
k+1 =
(
1.05 · ls
k,
|{Vi,j : Vij > τ s
k}| ≥ds,
0.95 · ls
k,
|{Vi,j : Vi,j > τ s
k}| < ds,
(17)
14


--- Page 15 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
and
lsmooth
k+1
=
(
1.05 · lsmooth
k
,
|{Vi,j : Vij > τ smooth
k
}| ≥ds + dsmooth,
0.95 · lsmooth
k
,
|{Vi,j : Vi,j > τ smooth
k
}| < ds + dsmooth,
(18)
where | · | denotes the cardinality of the set. Finally, we enforce the constraint lsmooth
k+1
←min{0.95ls
k+1, lsmooth
k+1 } to ensure
that the smoothing threshold remains strictly lower than the sharp threshold (lsmooth
k
< ls
k). Our approach eliminates the use
of inefficient top-k functions, thereby incurring almost no additional computational or temporal burden.
C. Experimental Details
Models. We utilize two popular classes of LLM models for our pre-training experiments:
• LLaMA. LLaMA (Touvron et al., 2023a) is a widely adopted dense decoder-only Transformer architecture. It employs
Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE) (Su et al., 2024), Swish-Gated Linear Units (SwiGLU), and Root Mean Square
Layer Normalization (RMSNorm). In this work, we pre-train LLaMA models with sizes ranging from 130M to 1.3B
parameters. Detailed model configurations are provided in Table 2.
• QwenMoE. Qwen2MoE (Yang et al., 2024) is a prominent open-source Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) decoder-only
Transformer. In contrast to LLaMA, Qwen2MoE integrates a hybrid attention mechanism (combining sliding window
and full attention) alongside its MoE architecture. For our experiments, we disable sliding window attention given
the relatively short context length. We configure the model to activate 4 experts per token. Auxiliary losses including
z-loss with coefficient 0.001 and load balancing loss with coefficient 0.01 are used to make sure stable training. Refer
to Table 3 for comprehensive configuration details.
Table 2. Dense model configurations and optimally-tuned peak learning rates for Muon and SOAP.
Acronym
Size
dmodel
dFF
n head
depth
lr max of Muon
lr max of SOAP
LLaMA (0.13B)
134M
768
2048
12
12
5e-3 (on C4)
-
LLaMA (0.25B)
247M
768
2560
16
24
3e-3 (on Pile)
3e-3 (on Pile)
LLaMA (0.5B)
518M
1280
3456
20
22
2e-3 (on Pile)
2e-3 (on Pile)
LLaMA (1.3B)
1339M
2048
5461
32
24
1e-3 (on Pile)
-
Table 3. MoE model configurations and optimally-tuned peak learning rates for Muon on Pile.
Acronym
Size
Activated Size
dmodel
dFF
n head
depth
n experts
lr max
QwenMoE (1B)
1040M
297M
768
3072
12
15
32
2e-3
Datasets.
We conduct pre-training on the following datasets:
• C4 (Raffel et al., 2020). The Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus (C4) dataset is employed for our small-to-medium scale
pre-training experiments. We use the T5 tokenizer with a vocabulary size of 32,100.
• The Pile (Gao et al., 2020). We utilize The Pile for large-scale (or larger-context) pre-training tasks. For this dataset,
we adopt the LLaMA-2 tokenizer (Touvron et al., 2023b) with a vocabulary size of 32,000.
Sequence Packing/Batching.
For the Pile dataset, we employ a standard sequence packing strategy: documents are
tokenized, concatenated into a continuous stream, and then segmented into fixed-length sequences of size L (depending on
the dataset). This approach minimizes padding overhead and ensures a near-constant number of effective tokens per batch.
Training progress is measured in terms of total tokens, with evaluation performed on the official validation splits.
LR schedulers. We evaluate two popular learning-rate (LR) scheduling strategies with 1,000 warm-up steps in all
pre-training experiments:
15


--- Page 16 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
• cos (cosine scheduler) (Touvron et al., 2023b): a linear warm-up to peak lr max, followed by cosine decay to a
terminal LR lr min. Following Hoffmann et al. (2022), we set lr min = 0.1 × lr max.
• wsd (warmup-stable-decay scheduler) (Hu et al., 2024; H¨agele et al., 2024): a linear warm-up to lr max, followed by
a stable phase keeping LR at lr max (up to 80% of total steps), and then a linear decay to 0 over the final 20% steps.
C.1. Experimental Details in Sections 5.2
In this subsection, we demonstrate that gradients (first-order information) can effectively approximate the top eigenspaces of
the Hessian (second-order information) for matrix block parameters in language models. Similar alignment phenomena
have been extensively investigated and verified in simplified models, both theoretically and empirically (Wang & Wu,
2023; Wu et al., 2022). To validate this in our context, we conduct a experiment on a small LLAMA2 model with
dmodel = 16, dmodel = 32, n head = 4, depth = 4, vocabulary size = 8, #params = 10, 640. The model is trained using
AdamW for 100 steps with a token batch size 8 × 8 = 64 and lr = 0.001. For Q,K,V,O, FFN (up, gate, down) blocks, we
compute the Hessian eigenvalue distribution within each block (Figures 2 and 8), test the alignment degree of each row’s
Hessian with G⊤G, and each column’s Hessian with GG⊤(Figures 2 and 7).

4
6
8
10
12
14
16

0.8

Figure 7. Hessian eigenvalue distribution of different blocks in the toy LLaMA2 model. The results of the up proj are in Figure 2.
Measure Coverage Degree between Two Subspaces.
Let A and B be two linear subspaces of Rd with dim(A) ≤dim(B).
Let PA = [u1, ..., uk1] ∈Rd×kA and PB = [v1, ..., vkB] ∈Rd×kB be the matrices consisting of the orthonormal bases of A
and B, respectively. We define the Coverage Score to quantify the extent to which A is contained within B:
Coverage Score(A, B) = 1
kA
∥P ⊤
A PB∥∗= 1
kA
kA
X
i=1
cos θi,
(19)
where ∥· ∥∗denotes the nuclear norm, and θi represent the i-th principal angle between subspaces A and B. By definition,
the Coverage Score lies in the interval [0, 1]. It represents the mean squared norm of the projection of A’s basis vectors onto
the subspace B. A score of 1 indicates that A ⊆B, while a score of 0 implies that A is orthogonal to B (A ⊥B). A value
closer to 1 suggests a higher degree of containment of A within B.
16


--- Page 17 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
-0.5
0
0.5
1
1.5
2
2.5
Eigenvalue (×10
4)
10
2
10
1
100
Normalized Frequency
Hessian Eigenvalues Distribution
(a) down proj

-2
-1

3)

Figure 8. Hessian eigenvalue distribution of different blocks in the toy LLaMA2 model. The results of the up proj are in Figure 2.
C.2. Experimental Details in Sections 6
Muon Baselines.
We adopt the standard Muon configuration described in (Liu et al., 2025a). Specifically, we apply Muon
specifically to 2D matrix blocks within transformer layers, while using AdamW for all other parameters (RMSNorm (norm),
Embedding (emb) and the Output (out)). We set the learning rate scaling factor to c = 0.2 max{m, n} for blocks that
use Muon with shape Rm×n, ensuring the update RMS norm aligns with that of AdamW. The baseline is configured with
Nesterov momentum using a decay coefficient θmuon = 0.95, weight decay λ = 0.1, and a gradient clipping threshold of 1.0.
SOAP Baselines.
We maintain the same weight decay and gradient clipping settings as in Muon. Following (Vyas et al.,
2025), we set the hyper-parameters to θ = 0.95, βv = 0.99, and k = 10. regarding the shampoo momentum, although
(Vyas et al., 2025) recommends βshampoo = 0.99, we compared βshampoo ∈{0.99, 0.95} and selected 0.95, as it yielded
lower validation losses in our experiments.
Learning Rate Schedules.
For the cos schedule, following Hoffmann et al. (2022), the minimum learning rate lr min
is set to 0.1 × lr max. For the wsd schedule, we allocate the warmup and stable phases to the first 80% of total iterations,
followed by a linear decay to 0 over the remaining 20%. We set the warm-up steps to 1000 for all pre-training experiments.
Additionally,
• C4 pre-training. We use a sequence length of 256 and a global batch size of 3,600. The total number of training
tokens is set to approximately 80 times the number of model parameters, corresponding to 4× the Chinchilla scaling
law (Hoffmann et al., 2022). The training process includes 1,000 warm-up steps. We perform a grid search for the
maximum learning rate lr max over the set {1e-3, 2e-3, 3e-3, 5e-3, 7e-3}. The resulting optimal learning
rates for each model are detailed in Table 2.
• Pile pre-training. We set the sequence length to 1,024. The batch size is set to 1,024 for the 0.25B and 0.5B models,
and 2,048 for the 1.3B model. The total number of training tokens is approximately 40 times the number of model
parameters (corresponding to 4× the Chinchilla scaling law (Hoffmann et al., 2022)), which includes 1,000 warm-up
steps. A grid search for lr max is performed over the set {7e-4, 1e-3, 2e-3, 3e-3, 5e-3, 7e-3}. The optimal
learning rates for each model are detailed in Tables 2 and 3.
17


--- Page 18 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
Hyper-parameter tuning for MUON-LITE.
For LLaMA models, we identified the optimal hyper-parameters {χ, β1, β2}
via grid search on the 250M model (Pile) and applied them uniformly to the 130M (C4), 500M (Pile), and 1.3B (Pile)
experiments. Specifically, fixing the base learning rate to Muon’s optimal value, we searched the sharp subspace dimension
ration rs ∈{0.1, 0.2}, and set ds = rs min{m, n} for q,k,v,o,ffn blocks of size Rm×n, and ds = rsmn for emb,
norm blocks, with all of them using the same ratio rs. The search space for other coefficients is β1 ∈{0.0, −0.25},
β2 ∈{0.5, 1.0, 2.0}, and χ ∈{2.0, 4.0}. For AdamW blocks, we set β1,2 = 0.0 for brevity, and dsmooth = 0.1mn (the
final loss is insensitive to this parameter). Following (Wang et al., 2025a), we exclude the output block from LITE (i.e.
treating the entire block as sharp directions), as accelerating it offers minimal gains and may risk instability. Finally, we
selected rs = 0.1 and the specific settings in LITE variants for LLaMA models are:
• MUON-LITE-H: (β1, β2) = (−0.25, 2.0).
• MUON-LITE-L: χ = 4 for emb, norm blocks and χ = 2 for Muon blocks.
• MUON-LITE: Adopts the χ settings from MUON-LITE-L and sets (β1, β2) = (−0.25, 1.0).
For MUON-LITE in the QwenMoE experiment, we set rs = 0.1 and β1 = 0.0. We searched χ ∈{4, 8} for emb and norm
blocks (while maintaining χ = 1 for others) and β2 ∈{1.0, 2.0} for blocks using Muon. The final configuration adopted
was χ = 8 (for emb/norm) and β2 = 1.0.
Hyper-parameter tuning for SOAP-LITE.
Similar to MUON-LITE. we identified the optimal hyper-parameters
{χ, β1, β2} via grid search on the 250M model (Pile) and applied them to other scenarios. Specifically, fixing the
base learning rate to SOAP’s optimal value, we searched the sharp subspace dimension ration rs ∈{0.1, 0.2}, and set
ds = rsmn for all blocks, with all of them using the same ratio rs. The search space for other coefficients is β1 = 0.0,
β2 ∈{0.25, 0.5, 1.0}, and χ ∈{2.0, 4.0}. We set dsmooth = 0.1mn uniformly (the final loss is insensitive to this parameter).
Following (Wang et al., 2025a), we exclude the output block from LITE (i.e. treating the entire block as sharp directions),
as accelerating it offers minimal gains and may risk instability. Finally, we selected rs = 0.2 and the specific settings in
SOAP-LITE variants for LLaMA models are:
• SOAP-LITE-H: (β1, β2) = (0.0, 0.5).
• SOAP-LITE-L: χ = 4 for emb, norm blocks and χ = 2 for Muon blocks.
• SOAP-LITE: Adopts the χ settings from SOAP-LITE-L and sets (β1, β2) = (0.0, 0.25).
D. Additional Experimental Results
D.1. Dense Models
Here we present the supplementary results for the experiments in Section 6.1.
We evaluate the 0-shot performance of the pre-trained LLaMA 1.3B models for 25k steps (Figure 4) in Table 4. MUON-LITE
demonstrates consistent improvements over the Muon baseline across the eight evaluated tasks. Notably, MUON-LITE
achieves a substantial gain on BoolQ and exhibits strong generalization on reasoning-heavy benchmarks, showing significant
margins on ARC-Challenge (+1.79) and MMLU (+1.39). These results indicate that MUON-LITE effectively enhances
model performance across diverse domains ranging from common sense reasoning to truthful QA.
Table 4. Evaluation results on downstream tasks (0-shot with lm-evaluation-harness) of LLaMA models (1.3B) pre-trained on Pile using
MUON-LITE and Muon. The best scores in each column are bolded. Abbreviations: T mc2=TruthfulQA mc2, AVG=Average score.
Method
ARC E
ARC C
PIQA
HellaSwag
BOOLQ
WinoGrande

T mc2
AVG
Muon
