Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
E. Discussions on Momentum Formulations and their Continuous-Time ODE Counterparts
E.1. Continuous-Time ODE Formulations
In this subsection, we introduce the continuous-time limits of momentum-based algorithms and demonstrate how to derive
discrete optimizers from these continuous formulations (modeled as second-order or first-order systems). The derivation
proceeds by reducing the second-order dynamics into a first-order system, followed by numerical discretization. This
pipeline serves as a foundation for the Riemannian setting (3), which is the primary focus of this work (see Appendix G).
We consider the inertial system with Hessian damping (ISHD) mentioned in Section 3.2
¨wt + αt ˙wt + βt∇2f(wt) ˙wt + γt∇f(wt) = 0,
(20)
where αt, βt, γt ≥0 denote the coefficients for momentum decay, Hessian damping, and the gradient driving force,
respectively. By introducing an auxiliary momentum variable mt to track the velocity ˙wt, we can reformulate this
second-order equation (20) into an equivalent first-order system:
(
˙wt = −γtmt −βt∇f(wt),
˙mt = −αtmt + (1 −αtβt/γt −˙βt)∇f(wt).
(21)
Applying a discretization with step size h > 0 yields the following update rule at the k-th iteration:
(
mk = (1 −αth)mk−1 + (1 −αkβk/γk −˙βk)h∇f(wk),
wk+1 = wk −γkhmk −βkh∇f(wk).
(22)
This formulation subsumes various momentum methods, recovering Heavy Ball momentum when βk = 0 and Nesterov-type
momentum when βk > 0. Crucially, the Hessian damping term βt∇2f(wt) ˙wt and its discretized counterpart βk∇f(wt)
serves to enhance standard Heavy Ball momentum by suppressing oscillations. We provide a detailed discussion in Appendix
E.2.
E.2. Heavy Ball Momentum, Nesterov Momentum, and Hessian Damping
In this section, we discuss equivalent formulations of Nesterov-type momentum and Heavy Ball momentum (also known as
Polyak momentum). Furthermore, we analyze the acceleration dynamics of Nesterov momentum, highlighting the intrinsic
connections between Hessian damping and gradient correction. For brevity, we assume the preconditioner is the identity
matrix (F = I) throughout this discussion.
Heavy Ball Momentum.
The Heavy Ball momentum update is defined as:
(
mk = (1 −α)mk−1 + ∇f(wk),
wk+1 = wk −ηmk.
(23)
This recurrence can be rewritten in a single-variable form equivalent to:
wk+1 = wk + (1 −α)(wk −wk−1) −η∇f (wk) .
(24)
This formulation aligns with the momentum schemes adopted in AdamW and many other adaptive optimizers. Note that
here, the coefficient α of the gradient term (typical in EMA-style updates) is absorbed into the effective learning rate η.
Nesterov Momentum.
Unfortunately, Heavy Ball momentum lacks a theoretical guarantee of globally achieving a faster
convergence rate than Gradient Descent, even in deterministic strongly convex settings. In contrast, Nesterov momentum
provably achieves acceleration: the convergence rates for Gradient Descent and Nesterov momentum are approximately
(1 −1
κ)k and (1 −
1
√κ)k respectively, where κ denotes the condition number.
Nesterov momentum with constant coefficients is typically formulated as:
(
xk = wk−1 −η∇f(wk−1),
wk = xk + (1 −α)(xk −xk−1).
(25)
19


--- Page 20 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
Eliminating the auxiliary variable x or w yields two equivalent interpretations of (25):
xk+1 = xk + (1 −α)(xk −xk−1) −η ∇f (xk + (1 −α)(xk −xk−1))
|
{z
}
gradient correction
,
(26)
and
wk+1 = wk + (1 −α)(wk −wk−1) −η∇f (wk) −(1 −α)η (∇f(wk) −∇f(wk−1))
|
{z
}
gradient correction
.
(27)
The Gradient Correction (Hessian Damping) Insight.
Comparing (27) with the Heavy Ball form (24), it is evident that
Nesterov momentum introduces an additional gradient correction term. Using a Taylor expansion, we observe that:
∇f(xk + (1 −α)(xk −xk−1)) ≈(1 −α)∇2f(xk)(xk −xk−1),
∇f(wk) −∇f(wk−1) ≈∇2f(wk)(wk −wk−1).
(28)
This reveals that the gradient correction implicitly incorporates Hessian information. This mechanism is the key to the
acceleration of Nesterov momentum: it exerts an inertial damping effect (often referred to as Hessian damping) which is
proportional to the curvature. Consequently, this term applies stronger damping in directions corresponding to large Hessian
eigenvalues, effectively suppressing oscillations.
The formulation in (27) is also mathematically equivalent to:
wk+1 = wk + (1 −α)(wk −wk−1) −η1(1 + αβ)∇f (wk) −βη1(1 −α) (∇f(wk) −∇f(wk−1))
|
{z
}
gradient correction
.
(29)
with η1 = η/(1 + αβ) and β = 1/(1 −α). Note that (29) can be transformed as:
(
mk = (1 −α)mk−1 + ∇f(wk),
wk+1 = wk −η1(mk + β∇f(wk)),
(30)
which corresponds to the discretized form discussed in Section 3.2.
We can observe that the term β∇f(wk) in (30) (i.e. the gradient correction term in (29)) arises from discretizing the Hessian
damping term β∇2f(wt) ˙wt in Eq. (1).
These insights lead to two key conclusions:
1. Implicit Hessian Awareness: Compared with Heavy ball momentum, Nesterov momentum introduces an additional
gradient correction term that implicitly encapsulates Hessian information (i.e. Hessian damping), which is instrumental
in achieving acceleration.
2. Continuous-Discrete Correspondence: This gradient correction term naturally arises from the discretization of the
Hessian damping term found in the continuous-time limiting flow.
Further Improving Nesterov momentum
Although Nesterov momentum leverages Hessian damping (via gradient
correction) to achieve acceleration, the coefficient β of this term is rigidly constrained by the momentum parameter (i.e.
β = 1/(1 −α)). It is therefore natural to relax this constraint and search for an optimal β, a direction supported by
recent theoretical results (Attouch et al., 2022; Xie et al., 2025). In this work, we adopt this generalized perspective and
incorporate richer second-order information, employing a larger β in flat directions than in sharp directions to further
enhance acceleration.
20


--- Page 21 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
E.3. Modeling AdEMAMix via a Third-Order ODE
The AdEMAMix optimizer (Pagliardini et al., 2025) builds upon the paradigm of AdamW by incorporating an additional
“slow” momentum term. At the k-th iteration, the parameter update rule is given by:
mfast
k
=(1 −α1)mfast
k−1 + α1gk,
mslow
k
=(1 −α2)mslow
k−1 + α2gk,
vk =(1 −βadam)vk−1 + βadamg⊙2
k
wk+1 =wk −ηk
mfast
k
+ κmslow
k
vk
,
(31)
where gk denotes the stochastic gradient. For brevity, we omit the weight decay and assume constant momentum hyper-
parameters. Typically, α1 is set to approximately 0.1, while α2 takes much smaller values, such as 10−4.
To derive its continuous-time version, we first simplify the analysis by omitting the preconditioner vk and treating the
learning rate ηk as a constant η. This yields the following system of differential equations:
d
dtmfast
t
= −α1mfast
k−1 + α1∇f(wk),
d
dtmslow
t
= −α2mslow
k−1 + α2∇f(wk),
d
dtwt = −η(mfast
t
+ κmslow
t
).
(32)
We note that applying a discretization with step size h = 1 to the above system recovers the update rules in (31). Next, we
compute the second and third-order time derivatives of the parameters, d2
dt2 wt and d3
dt3 wt:
d2
dt2 wt = −η(−α1mfast
t
−α2κmslow
t
+ (α1 + κα2)∇f(wt)),
d3
dt3 wt = −η(α2
1mfast
t
+ α2
2κmslow
t
−(α2
1 + κα2
2)∇f(wt) + (α1 + κα2)∇2f(wt) d
dtwt).
(33)
By combining (33) with the velocity equation d
dtwt = −η(mfast
t
+ κmslow
t
) to eliminate the momentum variables mfast
t
and mslow
t
, we arrive at the following third-order ODE :
d3
dt3 wt
| {z }
jerk
+(α1 + α2) d2
dt2 wt
| {z }
accelerated
velocity
+α1α2
d
dtwt
| {z }
velocity
+η(α1 + κα2) ∇2f(wt) d
dtwt
|
{z
}
Hessian damping
+η(1 + α1α2) ∇f(wt)
| {z }
gradient
= 0.
(34)
Similar to (3), the Riemannian counterpart of (34) can be derived as
∇˙wt∇˙wt ˙wt + (α1 + α2)∇˙wt ˙wt + α1α2
d
dt ˙wt + η(α1 + κα2) d
dt grad f(wt) + η(1 + α1α2) grad f(wt) = 0,
(35)
where we use Hess(wt) ˙wt = d
dt grad f(wt).
Intuitive Interpretation.
Standard momentum methods (like Heavy Ball) typically correspond to second-order ODEs,
describing a particle with mass and friction. In contrast, Eq. (34) reveals that AdEMAMix is governed by a third-order
dynamics. The term d3
dt3 wt introduces the concept of “jerk” (rate of change of accelerated velocity ¨wt) into the optimization
trajectory. The third-order structure with Hessian damping allows the optimizer to regulate not only velocity but also its
rate of change across multiple time scales. As a result, transient gradient fluctuations induced by rapidly varying curvature
are attenuated before influencing parameter updates, enabling more stable and robust navigation of highly ill-conditioned
landscapes.
21


--- Page 22 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
F. Foundations of Riemannian Geometry
In this section, we provide a detailed introduction to the foundation for Riemannian Geometry of (Rp, F) considered in
the main text. The parameter space is the Euclidean space Rp, while the metric is the Riemannian metric induced by the
preconditioner F(w), which is formally denoted as the manifold M = (Rp, F).
F.1. Riemannian Metric
The core structure of our framework is based on Riemannian metric, a smooth matrix-value map F : M = Rp →Sp
++ that
assigns an inner product to the tangent space TwM ∼= Rp at each point w ∈M. For any tangent vectors u, v ∈TwM, the
inner product is defined as ⟨u, v⟩F (w) := u⊤F(w)v. This metric induces a norm ∥u∥F (w) =
p
⟨u, u⟩F (w) in the tangent
space TwM, which measures the magnitude of perturbations u relative to the local curvature F(w).
While the tangent space TwM characterizes velocities or perturbations, its dual, the cotangent space T ∗
wM, consists of
differentials that act linearly on these velocities, naturally representing gradients of functions. Formally, T ∗
wM is the dual
space of TwM, consisting of all linear functionals ξ : TwM →R. It also holds that TwM ∼= Rp. The cotangent space
is equipped with the dual norm ∥u∥F (w)−1 =
p
u⊤F(w)−1u for u ∈T ∗M. In particular, the Riemannian metric F(w)
induces a natural isomorphism between the tangent and cotangent spaces:
F(w) : TwM →T ∗
wM,
F(w)−1 : T ∗
wM →TwM.
(36)
Under this identification, an element ξ ∈T ∗
wM acts as a linear functional on the tangent space via the Euclidean inner
product ξ(u) = ξ⊤u for u ∈TwM. Inner-product structures of TwM, T ∗
wM and between them are ⟨·, ·⟩F (w), ⟨·, ·⟩F (w)−1,
⟨·, ·⟩respectively.
Based on the Riemannian metric, we can define the length of a smooth curve γ : [0, 1] →M. The Riemannian length L(γ)
is given by integrating the local norm of the velocity vector ˙γ(t):
L(γ) =
Z 1
0
∥˙γ(t)∥F (γ(t))dt =
Z 1
0
q
˙γ(t)⊤F(γ(t))˙γ(t)dt.
(37)
The Riemannian distance (or geodesic distance) between two points x, y ∈M = Rp is defined as the infimum of lengths
over all piecewise smooth curves connecting them:
dF (x, y) = inf
γ∈D L(γ),
(38)
where D is the set containing all smooth curves γ connecting (x, y) with γ(0) = x, γ(1) = y.
The distance reduces to the Euclidean metric when F = I. Furthermore, when (x, y) are sufficiently close locally, we have
dF (x, y) ≈∥x −y∥F (x). The Riemannian gradient grad f is an example of vector field.
Smooth Vector Fields and the Tangent Bundle.
With the tangent space TwM defined at each point, we consider the
collection of all tangent vectors across the manifold, known as the tangent bundle, denoted by TM := S
w∈M({w}×TwM).
The tangent bundle is itself a smooth manifold of dimension 2p. A vector field V is a mapping (formally called a section)
that assigns to every point w ∈M a specific tangent vector V (w) ∈TwM. The space of smooth vector fields, denoted by
C∞(M, TM), consists of all such fields V where the assignment varies smoothly with w. In the global coordinate system
of M ∼= Rp, any vector field V ∈C∞(M, TM) can be uniquely expressed as a linear combination of the coordinate basis
vectors {
∂
∂w1 , . . . ,
∂
∂wp } (which form the standard basis for each TwM):
V (w) =
p
X
i=1
vi(w) ∂
∂wi
,
(39)
where each component function vi : M →R is a smooth scalar function. For a function f, (V f)|w := V (w)f(w) =
Pp
i=1 vi(w)
∂
∂wi f(w) and V Uf := V (Uf) for vector field U.
F.2. The Levi-Civita Connection
In Euclidean spaces, comparing vectors at different points is trivial because the tangent spaces are identical. On a curved
manifold, tangent spaces TxM and TyM are distinct inner-product spaces. To differentiate vector fields, we require an
affine connection ∇, which provides a rule for connecting adjacent tangent spaces.
22


--- Page 23 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
Definition.
The Levi-Civita connection ∇(·)(·) : C∞(M, TM) × C∞(M, TM) →C∞(M, TM) is the fundamental
connection associated with the metric F. It is uniquely determined by two geometric conditions:
1. Torsion-Free: For any vector fields X, Y ∈C∞(M, TM), the connection is symmetric: ∇XY −∇Y X = [X, Y ],
where [X, Y ] is the Lie bracket (in Rp, defined by [X, Y ] = XY −Y X).
2. Metric Compatibility: The connection preserves the inner product structure. Formally, for vector fields X, Y, Z:
X⟨Y, Z⟩F = ⟨∇XY, Z⟩F + ⟨Y, ∇XZ⟩F .
(40)
Coordinate Representation (Christoffel Symbols).
Since our manifold is globally Rp, we can express the covariant
derivative explicitly using the standard basis {e1, . . . , ep}. Let Γk
ij(w) denote the Christoffel symbols of the second kind.
The covariant derivative of a vector field v along u is given component-wise by:
(∇uv)k =
p
X
i=1
ui
∂vk
∂wi
+
X
i,j≤p
Γk
ij(w)uivj.
(41)
The first term captures the standard Euclidean directional derivative, while the second term corrects for the twisting of the
coordinate system induced by the metric. The Christoffel symbols are derived from the metric F(w):
Γk
ij = 1
2
X
l
(F −1)kl
∂Fjl
∂wi
+ ∂Fil
∂wj
−∂Fij
∂wl

.
(42)
Here, (F −1)kl denotes the (k, l)-entry of the inverse metric matrix, and indices denote partial derivatives with respect to
coordinates. The Levi-connection would reduce to the Euclidean directional derivative when F = I, as Γk
ij = 0 in this case.
F.3. Riemannian Gradient and Hessian
With the metric and connection defined, we can rigorously define the following differential operators.
Riemannian Gradient.
The Riemannian gradient gradf(w) is the unique tangent vector representing the differential
df(w) via the metric:
⟨gradf(w), u⟩F (w) = df(w)[u] := lim
ϵ→0
f(w + ϵu) −f(w)
ϵ
for any u ∈Rp.
(43)
It has the closed form
gradf(w) = F(w)−1∇f(w),
(44)
where ∇f(w) is the standard Euclidean gradient. This confirms that the preconditioner F −1 acts as the “inverse metric”
mapping cotangent vectors (gradients) to tangent vectors (directions).
Riemannian Hessian.
The Riemannian Hessian is a linear operator on the tangent space, defined as the covariant derivative
of the gradient vector field:
Hessf(w)[u] = ∇ugradf(w),
∀u ∈TwM.
(45)
Unlike the Euclidean Hessian ∇2f(w), the Riemannian Hessian accounts for the curvature of the manifold. Explicitly:
Hessf(w) = F(w)−1 ␀∇2f(w) −K(w)

,
(46)
where
[K(w)]ij =
p
X
k=1
Γk
ij(w) ∂f
∂wk
(w).
When F(w) changes slowly with w, we have Γk
ij ≈0 and Hessf(w) ≈F(w)−1∇2f(w).
23


--- Page 24 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
G. Transforming the Second Order RISHD (the Riemannian ODE Framework) into a First
Order System
Lemma G.1. Let M = Rp be endowed with a Riemannian metric tensor F(w) ≻0. For any vector fields u, v ∈
C∞(M, TM), we have the following expression for the Levi-Civita connection:
∇uv =
∂uv
|{z}
Euclidean
derivative
+ 1
2F −1 
(∂uF)v + (∂vF)u −∇w(u⊤Fv)

|
{z
}
Geometric correction
.
(47)
Proof. By Koszul formula (Corollary 5.11 in (Lee, 2018)), we have
2 ⟨∇uv, h⟩F = ∂u ⟨v, h⟩F + ∂v ⟨u, h⟩F −∂h ⟨u, v⟩F + ⟨[u, v], h⟩F −⟨[u, h], v⟩F −⟨[v, h], u⟩F .
(48)
Substituting
∂u(v⊤Fh) = (∂uv)⊤Fh + v⊤(∂uF)h + v⊤F(∂uh),
∂v(u⊤Fh) = (∂vu)⊤Fh + u⊤(∂vF)h + u⊤F(∂vh),
−∂h(u⊤Fv) = −(∂hu)⊤Fv −u⊤(∂hF)v −u⊤F(∂hv),
[u, v] = ∂uv −∂vu,
[u, h] = ∂uh −∂hu,
[v, h] = ∂vh −∂hv,
(49)
into (48) yields
2h⊤F∇uv = h⊤
2F∂uv + (∂uF)v + (∂vF)u −∇w(u⊤Fv)

.
(50)
We get the conclusion.
Proposition G.2. Let M = Rd be endowed with a Riemannian metric tensor F(w) ≻0. Then the RISHD
∇˙wt ˙wt + αt ˙wt + βt∇˙wt grad f(wt) + γt grad f(wt) = 0.
(51)
with αt = α −˙ηt/ηt, βt = βηt, γt = ηt(αβ + 1) is equivalent to the following first order system:
(
˙wt = −ηtF(wt)−1(mt + β∇f(wt)),
˙mt = −αmt + ∇f(wt) + Rt,
(52)
where we define ut = mt + β∇f(wt) and
Rt = −ηt
2 ∇w(u⊤
t F(wt)ut) + βηt
2
␀∇F(wt)[F −1(wt)ut]F −1(wt)∇f(wt) −∇F(wt)[F −1(wt)∇f(wt)]F −1(wt)ut

+ βηt
2 ∇F(wt)[F −1(wt)ut]F −1(wt)∇f(wt).
(53)
Remark G.3. (53) is equivalent to (3) as ∇˙wt grad f(wt) = Hess(wt) ˙wt.
Proof. First, we let mt = −F(wt) ˙wt/ηt −β∇f(wt) and get
˙wt = −ηtF(wt)−1(mt + β∇f(wt)),
¨wt = −˙ηtF(wt)−1(mt + β∇f(wt)) −ηt
d
dtF(wt)−1(mt + β∇f(wt)) −ηtF(wt)−1( ˙mt + β∇2f(wt) ˙wt).
(54)
By Lemma G.1, we have
∇˙wt ˙wt = ¨wt + F(wt)−1 ˙F(wt) ˙wt −1
2F −1(wt)∇w( ˙w⊤
t F(w) ˙wt)|w=wt,
(55)
24


--- Page 25 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
and
∇˙wt grad f(wt) =∇˙wt(F −1(wt)∇f(wt))
= d
dt(F −1(wt))∇f(wt) + F −1(wt)∇2f(wt) ˙wt + 1
2F −1(wt) d
dtF(wt)F −1(wt)∇f(wt)
+ 1
2F −1(wt)∇F(wt)[F −1(wt)∇f(wt)] ˙wt −1
2F −1(wt)∇w( ˙w⊤
t F(w)F −1(wt)∇f(wt)|w=wt
=F −1(wt)∇2f(wt) ˙wt −1
2F −1(wt) d
dtF(wt)F −1(wt)∇f(wt)
+ 1
2F −1(wt)∇F(wt)[F −1(wt)∇f(wt)] ˙wt −1
2F −1(wt)∇w( ˙w⊤
t F(w)F −1(wt)∇f(wt)|w=wt.
(56)
Substituting them into (51) yields
˙mt = −αmt + ∇f(wt) −ηt
2 ∇w(u⊤
t F(wt)ut) −β
2 ∇F(wt)[ ˙wt]F −1(wt)∇f(wt) + β
2 ∇F(wt)[F −1(wt)∇f(wt)] ˙wt
−β
2 ∇F(wt)[ ˙wt]F −1(wt)∇f(wt).
(57)
We get the conclusion.
Remark G.4. In general, we assume that F(w) varies slowly with respect to w to ensure the stability of the preconditioner.
This assumption is practically justified by the common use of Exponential Moving Average (EMA) with a decay coefficient
close to 1, which enforces a smooth evolution of the estimated preconditioner. Consequently, based on the expression of Rt
in (53), this term is typically negligible relative to mt and ∇f(wt), as it scales with the small step size ηt and the limited
variation ∥∇F(w)∥op.
H. Theoretical Analysis on Quadratic Objectives: An Illustrative Example
To elucidate the acceleration mechanism of LITE, we analyze the discrete-time dynamics of the update rule (2), which
corresponds to non-accelerated adaptive optimizers, on a quadratic objective function:
f(w) = 1
2
p
X
i=1
λiw2
i + b⊤w.
(58)
We assume the eigenvalues are sorted as λ1 > · · · > λk > 0 ≥· · · ≥λp, representing an anisotropic landscape where
negative λi correspond to local nonconvex directions. For simplicity, we set the step size h = 1 and the preconditioner
F(w) = Ip. Since the Hessian is diagonal, the dynamics decouple across coordinates.
Let w⋆= −[b1/λ1, . . . , bp/λp]⊤denote the stationary point. To characterize the trajectory of the error term ek = wk −w⋆,
we eliminate the auxiliary momentum variable mk. From (2) with F = Ip, the i-th coordinate of mk can be expressed in
terms of ek as:
η(mk)i = (1 −ηβλi)(ek)i −(ek+1)i,
1 ≤i ≤p.
(59)
Applying a time shift to (59), we obtain:
η(mk−1)i = (1 −ηβλi)(ek−1)i −(ek)i.
(60)
Combining these relations leads to a homogeneous second-order linear recurrence:
(ek+1)i −2Ti(ek)i + Di(ek−1)i = 0,
(61)
where the coefficients are defined as:
Ti = T(λi) := 1 −α + ηλi(β + 1)
2
,
Di = D(λi) := (1 −α)(1 −ηβλi).
(62)
25


--- Page 26 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
The characteristic equation is given by r2 −2Tir + Di = 0, with roots:
ri,1,2 = Ti ±
q
T 2
i −Di.
(63)
Based on the discriminant ∆i = T 2
i −Di, the dynamics fall into three regimes, yielding the following closed-form solutions
(ci,1, ci,2 are constants determined by initialization):
wk,i = −bi
λi
+





ci,1rk
i,1 + ci,2rk
i,2,
∆i > 0 (Overdamped),
(ci,1 + ci,2k)Dk/2
i
,
∆i = 0 (Critically damped),
Dk/2
i
[ci,1 cos(kθi) + ci,2 sin(kθi)] ,
∆i < 0 (Underdamped),
(64)
where θi = arccos(Ti/√Di).
Stability Analysis.
Convergence in convex directions requires the magnitude of all characteristic roots to be at most
1. According to the Jury Stability Criterion, the necessary and sufficient conditions are |Pi(0)| ≤1, Pi(1) ≥0, and
Pi(−1) ≥0, where Pi(z) = z2 −2Tiz + Di. Solving these inequalities yields the stability bound:
η ≤
2(2 −α)
λ1 max{1 + 2β −αβ, 2β(1 −α)}.
(65)
Ideally, this condition confirms that the maximum allowable step size is governed by the sharpest direction λ1.
Regime Classification.
We next analyze how the curvature λi determines the dynamic regime. The equation T(λ)2 =
D(λ) with respect to λ:

1 −α + ηλ(β + 1)
2
2
= (1 −α)(1 −ηβλ)
(66)
possesses two real positive roots, denoted ˜λ1 < ˜λ2. By the properties of quadratic functions, we refer eigenvalues λ
satisfying λ < ˜λ1 to flat directions (parts), which fall into the overdamped regime. Conversely, eigenvalues λ ≥˜λ1
correspond to sharp directions, whose dynamic regimes depend on the specific magnitude of the curvature (typically
transitioning into the underdamped regime).
Acceleration Mechanism of LITE.
Here we suppose that the stability condition (65) holds. Since LITE explicitly
increases η and β in flat directions, we focus our analysis on the regime λi < ˜λ1. In this regime, the dynamics are
overdamped, and dominated by the larger root r1 = T +
√
T 2 −D. We demonstrate that our parameter adjustment modifies
r1 to facilitate escape from nonconvex regions and foster convergence in convex regions.
We analyze the sensitivity of r1 to β and η case by case:
• Case 1: Flat Nonconvex Directions (λ < 0). The coefficients become T = 1 −α
2 + η|λ|(β+1)
2
and D = (1 −α)(1 +
ηβ|λ|). Clearly, T > 1 −α, and both T and D are strictly increasing functions of β and η. Consider the partial
derivatives:
∂r1
∂β = ∂T
∂β +
1
2
√
T 2 −D

2T ∂T
∂β −∂D
∂β

.
(67)
Since T > 0, and noting that:
2T ∂T
∂β −∂D
∂β = Tη|λ| −(1 −α)η|λ| > 0,
(68)
(and similarly for η), we conclude that increasing β or η yields a strictly larger r1 (with r1 > 1). This increases the
exponential divergence rate along negative curvature directions, thereby facilitating rapid escape.
• Case 2: Flat Convex Directions (0 < λ < ˜λ1). In this regime, the convergence rate is governed by how quickly
r1 decays to zero. To accelerate convergence, we must minimize r1. The coefficients are T = 1 −α+ηλ(β+1)
2
and
D = (1 −α)(1 −ηβλ). Observing the monotonicity:
∂T
∂β < 0,
∂D
∂β < 0
(and similarly for η).
(69)
26


--- Page 27 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
Both T and D decrease monotonically as β or η increases. Since r1 is strictly increasing with respect to T and D,
the dominant root r1 decreases when β (or η) increases. This reduction in the spectral radius directly fosters faster
convergence.
I. Some Common Adaptive Optimizers for LLM Pre-training
Here we list some common optimizers in LLM pre-training. They can be subsumed by discretizing the ODE framework (2).
We omit gradient clipping and AdamW-type bias correction coefficient
1−θt
√
1−βtv for notational brevity.
Algorithm 2 AdamW
Require: Hyper-parameters: θ, βv, ϵ, {ηt}, λ
Initialize m = 0, v = 0, t = 0
for t = 1, 2, . . . do
Compute stochastic gradient gt at current parameters wt.
mt = θmt−1 + (1 −θ)gt
vt = βvvt−1 + (1 −βv)g⊙2
t
wt+1 = wt −ηt

mt
√vt+ϵ + λwt

end for
Algorithm 3 Lion
Require: θ, βv, {ηt}, ϵ, λ
Initialize m = 0
for t = 1, 2, . . . do
Compute gradient gt at current parameters wt
ut = θmt−1 + (1 −θ)gt
mt = βvmt−1 + (1 −βv)gt
wt+1 = wt −ηt sign(ut) −ηtλwt
end for
Algorithm 4 MARS
Require: θ, βv, γ, ϵ, {ηt}, λ
Initialize m = 0, v = 0, g0 = 0
for t = 1, 2, . . . do
Compute stochastic gradient gt at current parameters wt
ct = gt + γ
θ
1 −θ(gt −gt−1)
mt = θmt−1 + (1 −θ)ct
vt = βvvt−1 + (1 −βv)c⊙2
t
wt+1 = wt −ηt
mt
√vt + ϵ −ηtλwt
end for
Remark I.1. In MARS, the momentum term is updated as:
mt = θmt−1 + (1 −θ)gt + γθ(gt −gt−1).
(70)
It can be transformer to the formulation in (2) as:
st = θst−1 + gt,
˜mt = st +
γ
(1 −γ)(1 −θ)gt,
(71)
with mt = (1 −γ)(1 −θ) ˜mt. Note that γ ̸= 1; otherwise, (70) would reduce to mt = θmt−1 −θgt−1, thereby completely
discarding the current gradient information gt.
27


--- Page 28 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
Algorithm 5 Muon
Require: θmuon, η, ϵ, θadamw, βv, {ηt}, λ
Initialize state m
for t = 1, 2, . . . do
For weights in Output, Embedding, and Norm layers:
Update using AdamW with hyper-parameters θadamw, βv, ϵ, ηt, λ.
For weight matrices in transformer layers:
Compute stochastic gradient gt at current parameters wt
mt = θmuonmt−1 + gt
ut = θmuonmt + gt
wt+1 = wt −scale ·ηt NS(ut) −ηtλwt, where scale = 0.2
p
max{m, n} for w ∈Rm×n.
end for
Algorithm 6 SOAP
Require: θ, βv, βshampoo, k, ϵ, λ
Initialize state: m = 0, v = 0, L = 0, R = 0, Ql, Qr
for t = 1, 2, . . . do
For weights in Output, Embedding, and Norm layers:
Update using AdamW with hyper-parameters β1, β2, ϵ, ηt, λ.
for weight matrices in transformer layers: do
Compute stochastic gradient Gt at current parameters Wt
Grot
t
= Q⊤
l GtQr
Mt = θMt−1 + (1 −θ)Gt
Vt = βvVt−1 + (1 −βv)(Grot
t )⊙2
Wt+1 = Wt −ηtQl
Q⊤
l MtQr
√Vt + ϵ

Q⊤
r −ηtλWt
Lt = βshampooLt−1 + (1 −βshampoo)GtG⊤
t
Rt = βshampooRt−1 + (1 −βshampoo)G⊤
t Gt
if t mod k = 0 then
Ql ←QR(LtQl) {QR decomposition}
Qr ←QR(RtQr)
end if
end for
end for
I.1. Exact Preconditioner (Riemannian Metrics) Forms for Common Adaptive Optimizers
In this section, we analyze the implicit metric structure F induced by the preconditioner estimation (update) scheme for
each parameter block, and compare the way in approximating ˆF(w) = (E[gg⊤])1/2 in Section 4.1.
Let G ∈Rm×n denote the stochastic gradient in matrix form, and let g = vec(G) ∈Rmn be its vectorized counterpart.
The Fisher-type metric ˆF(w) = (E[gg⊤])1/2 ∈Rmn×mn is often referred to the Whitening metric (Yang & Laaksonen,
2008; Frans et al., 2025), which has demonstrated efficient and stable performance in neural network training. Below,
we demonstrate how practical inverse preconditioners Fk in common adaptive optimizers approximate their correspond-
ing deterministic forms F(wk), which in turn serve as approximations of ˆF(wk). Throughout, we utilize the identity
vec(ABC) = (C⊤⊗A) vec(B).
EMA-based Preconditioners.
AdamW employs an Exponential Moving Average (EMA) to update its preconditioner
F −1
k
via Fk = diag v1/2
k
with vk = βvvk−1 + (1 −βv)gk ⊙gk . This expands to vk = (1 −βv) Pk
i=0 βk−i
v
gi ⊙gi, where
βv is typically close to 1. Observing that (1 −βv) Pk
i=k−t βk−i
v
≈1 for a large window t, and assuming that the stochastic
gradients gi are approximately independent samples while ˆF(w) changes slowly with respect to w, we can deduce that v1/2
k
provides a stable and efficient approximation of F adam(w) = diag ˆF(w). This approximation logic can directly extend to
28


--- Page 29 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
other optimizers that accumulate preconditioners via EMA, such as SOAP. Specifically, for SOAP, from Algorithm 6 we
have
Fk = (Qr ⊗Ql) (diag vec(Vk))
1
2 (Qr ⊗Ql)⊤, with Vk = βvVk−1 + (1 −βv)(Grot
k )⊙2,
(72)
where Ql, Qr are updated in a lazy fashion, and Grot
k = Q⊤
l GkQr. This gives
F soap(w) = (Qr ⊗Ql)
␀diag E[grotg⊤
rot]
 1
2 (Qr ⊗Ql)⊤,
(73)
where Ql and Qr are the eigenvectors of E[GG⊤] and E[G⊤G], respectively, and grot = vec(Q⊤
l GQr). Thus, F soap(w)
approximates ˆF(w) via a structured, two-sided Kronecker factorization.
Momentum-based Preconditioners.
We take Muon as a representative example. At the k-th iteration, the momentum
is updated as Mk = θmuonMk−1 + Gk, where Gk ∈Rm×n (assuming m ≥n). We have Fk = (M ⊤
k Mk)1/2 ⊗Im (or
replacing Mk with its Nesterov-accelerated version). Expanding this recurrence yields Mk = Pk
i=0 θk−i
muonGi. Analyzing
the second moment, we have:
E[M ⊤
k Mk] =
X
i,j≤k
θ2k−(i+j)
muon
E[G⊤
i Gj] ≈
k
X
i=0
θ2(k−i)
muon E[G⊤
i Gi].
(74)
Here, we assume that in the middle to late stages of training, the deterministic gradient component is negligible compared to
the stochastic noise (or second moment magnitude), and that gradient noise is approximately independent across different
steps i, j (i ̸= j). Applying the same EMA perspective as used for AdamW, M ⊤
k Mk serves as a stable estimator of
1
1−θ2muon E[G⊤G]. Similarly, for the Nesterov-corrected momentum f
Mk = Mk + Gk/(1 −θmuon), we can similarly get
that f
M ⊤
k f
Mk approximates cE[G⊤G] for some constant scaling factor c. Consequently, the implicit metric for Muon can be
characterized as F muon(w) ∝(E[G⊤G])
1
2 ⊗Im, representing a single-sided Kronecker-factored approximation of ˆF(w).
This derivation extends naturally to element-wise operations on momentum; for instance, Lion’s update rule implies a metric
F lion(w) ∝diag( ˆF(w)).
J. Proof
Notations
A map is said to be smooth if it is infinitely differentiable. For notational brevity, we define Fs(w) =
Ps(w)F(w)Ps(w), Ff(w) = Pf(w)F(w)Pf(w) and the corresponding pseudo inverse F †
s (w) = Ps(w)F −1(w)Ps(w),
F †
f (w) = Pf(w)F −1(w)Pf(w). Denote κFs := ρ/λFs, κF := ρ/λF . The full version of Assumption 7.2.2 concerning the
operator norm bounds on ∇Ps and ∇F is as follows. For any u, v, w ∈Rp, we have
∥∇Ps(w)[u]v∥F (w) ≤δ
G∥u∥F (w)∥v∥F (w),
∥∇F(w)[u]v∥F −1(w) ≤δF
G ∥u∥F (w)∥v∥F (w).
(75)
J.1. Landscape Analysis
In this subsection we mainly focus on properties of Φ and R.
Lemma J.1. Suppose that Assumptions 7.2 holds. Define δFs = 2δ + δF . Then we have
max{∥∇F †
s (w)[u]v∥F (w), ∥∇F †
f (w)[u]v∥F (w)} ≤δFs
G ∥u∥F (w)∥v∥F −1(w),
(76)
max{∥∇Fs(w)[u]v∥F (w), ∥∇Ff(w)[u]v∥F (w)} ≤δFf
G ∥u∥F (w)∥v∥F (w).
(77)
29


--- Page 30 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
Proof. By (75), we have
v⊤∇(PsF −1Ps)(w)[u]v =2v⊤∇Ps(w)[u]F −1(w)Ps(w)v + v⊤Ps(w)∇(F −1)(w)[u]Ps(w)v
≤2∥v∥F (w)−1∥∇Ps(w)[u]F(w)−1Ps(w)v∥F (w)
+ ∥Ps(w)F(w)−1v∥F (w)∥∇F(w)[u]F(w)−1Ps(w)v∥F (w)−1
≤
δFs
∥∇f(w)∥F (w)−1 ∥v∥F (w)−1∥v∥F †
s (w)∥u∥F (w),
(78)
where the first inequality uses ∇(F −1)(w)[u] = −F −1(w)∇F(w)[u]F −1(w).
Similarly, for any p,
p⊤∇(PsFPs)(w)[u]v
=p⊤∇Ps(w)[u]F(w)Ps(w)v + p⊤Ps(w)F(w)∇Ps(w)[u]v + p⊤Ps(w)∇F(w)[u]Ps(w)v
≤∥∇Ps(w)[u]v∥F (w)∥F(w)Ps(w)p∥F (w)−1 + ∥∇Ps(w)[u]p∥F (w)∥F(w)Ps(w)v∥F (w)−1
+ ∥Ps(w)p∥F (w)∥∇F(w)[u]Ps(w)v∥F (w)−1
≤δ(∥v∥Fs(w)∥p∥F (w) + ∥v∥F (w)∥p∥Fs(w)) + δF ∥v∥Fs(w)∥p∥Fs(w)
∥∇f(w)∥F (w)−1
∥u∥F (w).
(79)
Using ∇Pf = −∇Ps, we can similarly get
v⊤∇(PfF −1Pf)(w)[u]v ≤
δFs
∥∇f(w)∥F (w)−1 ∥v∥F (w)−1∥v∥F †
f (w)∥u∥F (w),
(80)
and
v⊤∇(PfFPf)(w)[u]v ≤
δFs
∥∇f(w)∥F (w)−1 ∥v∥F (w)∥v∥Ff (w)∥u∥F (w).
(81)
Lemma J.2. Suppose Assumptions 7.1 and 7.2 hold. For any w ∈U, we have
∇Φ(w)Ps(w)F −1(w)∇f(w) = 0,
(82)
and Range ∇Φ(w) ⊂TΦ(w)R. Especially, if we further assume that max{δF , δ} < 1
3λHF,s, then for any z ∈R, ∇Φ(z)
is the oblique projection onto TzR in the direct sum decomposition Rp = TzR ⊕Range Ps(z).
Proof. First, note that Φ(w) = limn→∞ϕ(n)
1 (w), where ϕ1(w) := ψt(w)|t=1 denotes the time-1 map of the flow. Since F
and f are smooth, ϕ1 is smooth. Then the smoothness of Φ follows immediately from Theorem 5.1 in (Falconer, 1983). For
any w ∈U, noting that
−∇Φ(w)Ps(w)F −1(w)∇f(w) = d
dtΦ(ψt(w))|t=0 = d
dtΦ(w)|t=0 = 0,
(83)
we get (82). Besides, Range ∇Φ(w) ⊂TΦ(w)R is due to Φ(w) ∈R.
It follows that for any z ∈R and any v ∈Rp, we have
0 =∇
␀∇ΦF †
s ∇f

(z)[v]
=∇2Φ(z)[v] F †
s (z)∇f(z)
|
{z
}
=0
+∇Φ(z)∇F †
s (z)[v]∇f(z) + ∇Φ(z)F †

=∇Φ(z)
␀∇F †
s (z)[v]∇f(z) + F †
s (z)∇2f(z)[v]

.
(84)
Define the linear map Tz as Tz(v) = ∇F †
s (z)[v]∇f(z) + F †
s (z)∇2f(z)v. Noting that
∇F †
s (z)[v]∇f(z) =∇Ps(z)[v]F −1(z)Ps(z)∇f(z) + Ps(z)∇F −1(z)[v]Ps(z)∇f(z) + Ps(z)F −1(z)∇Ps(z)[v]∇f(z)
=Ps(z)F −1(z)∇Ps(z)[v]∇f(z),
(85)

Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
the range of Tz is a subspace of Range Ps(z). By Lemma J.1 and Assumption 7.2, for v satisfying Ps(z)v = v,
∥Tz(v)∥F (z)−1 ≥λHF,s∥v∥F (z) −δFs
G ∥∇f(z)∥F −1(z)∥v∥F (z) ≥(λHF,s −δFs)∥v∥F (z).
(86)
This implies that Tz|Range Ps(z) : Range Ps(z) →Range Ps(z) is a linear isomorphism. Therefore, by (84), we get that
Range Ps(z) ⊂Null ∇Φ(z), and dim(Null ∇Φ(z)) ≥dim(Range Ps(z)).
On the other hand, for any smoothed curve ˜zt on R, ∇Φ(˜zt) ˙˜zt =
d
dtΦ(˜zt) = ˙˜zt. Thus ∇Φ(z)|TzR = id for any
z ∈R. It yields p = dim(Range ∇Φ(z)) + dim(Null ∇Φ(z)) ≥dim(TzR) + dim(Range Ps(z)) ≥p, implying that
Range Ps(z) = Null ∇Φ(z). We get the conclusion.
By definition, f exhibits strong convexity along the sharp directions, and consequently inherits certain properties of strongly
convex functions. We first present this characteristic (as well as L-smoothness) in Lemmas J.3 and J.4.
Lemma J.3. Suppose Assumptions 7.1 and 7.2 hold. Denote γ = λHF ,s −δFs
2 , ℓ= LHF ,s + δFs
2 . Then for any w ∈U the
following inequality hold
∥Ps(ψt(w))F(ψt(w))−1∇f(ψt(w))∥F (ψt(w)) ≤exp(−γt)∥Ps(w)F(w)−1∇f(w)∥F (w),
(87)
and

(88)
Proof.

The following lemma elucidates the property of ∇Φ: it approximates the projection to the flat subspace.
Lemma J.5. Suppose Assumptions 7.1 and 7.2 hold, and
µ1 := λHF ,s −3

Then for any w ∈U and any vector v ∈Rp, we have
∥Ps(Φ(w))∇Φ(w)v∥F (Φ(w)) ≤ϵΦ
2 ∥v∥F (Φ(w)),
(100)
∥Pf(Φ(w))∇Φ(w)v −Pf(w)v∥F (Φ(w)) ≤ϵΦ
2 ∥v∥F (Φ(w)).
(101)
Thus
∥∇Φ(w)v −Pf(w)v∥F (Φ(w)) ≤ϵΦ∥v∥F (Φ(w)).
(102)
Proof. Define
ht = Ps(ψt(w))∂wψt(w)[v],
rt = Pf(ψt(w))∂wψt(w)[v].
(103)
It implies that ht + rt = ∂wψt(w)[v], h0 + r0 = v and limt→∞ht + limt→∞rt = ∇Φ(w)[v].

Remark J.7. Such choices of α and β1 yield fast convergence in the (strongly convex) sharp directions of the ill-conditioned
landscape. The rate exp


R t

Proof. We first consider general hyper-parameters α, β1 and ηt. Recall that ˙wt = −ηtFs(wt)−1(mt + β1∇f(wt)) −

Lemma J.8. Suppose Assumptions 7.1 and 7.2 hold. For any z ∈R, we have
∥PR(z)v −Pf(z)v∥F (z) ≤
δFs
λHF ,s
∥v∥F (z).
(135)
Proof. We first compute the closed form of PR. Let 0p denote the p dimensional all zero vector. Note that the p −k
dimensional manifold R is defined by R = {w : Ps(w)∇f(w) = 0p} = {w : F †
s (w)∇f(w) = 0p}. Define N(w) =
F †
s (w)∇f(w). Thus the tangent space is TzR = {v : ∇N(z)v = 0p}. The projection to TzM is given by
PR(z)v = argminu∈Rp ∥v −u∥2
F (z),
s.t. ∇N(z)u = 0p.
(136)
Directly computing ∇N gives ∇N(z)u = ∇F †
s (w)[u]∇f(w) + F †
s (w)∇f 2(w)u.
Noting that ∥v −u∥2
F (z) = ∥F(z)
1
2 v −F(z)
1
2 u∥2
2 and ∇N(z)u = ∇N(z)F(z)−1
2 F(z)
1
2 u, We have
F(z)
1
2 PR(z)v = (I −F(z)−1
2 ∇N(z)⊤(∇N(z)F(z)−1∇N(z)⊤)†∇N(z)F(z)−1
2
|
{z
}
K(z)
)F(z)
1
2 v.
(137)
For notational brevity, we define J = F(z)−1
2 Ps(z)∇2f(z)Ps(z)F(z)−1
2 , and ∆J ∈Rk×p satisfying ∆Jq =
F(z)
1
2 ∇F †
s (w)[F(z)−1
2 q]⊤∇f(z) for any q ∈Rp.
Note that K(z) is the Euclidean projection to the kernel subspace:
ker

∇N(z)F(z)−1
2

= ker

F(z)
1
2 ∇N(z)F(z)−1
2

= ker (J + ∆J) ,
(138)
and Pf(z) is the Euclidean projection to kerJ. Using Wedin sin(Θ) Theorem (Lemma J.17) to J and J + ∆J, we get
∥K(z) −Pf(z)∥2 ≤∥∆J∥2
λHF ,s
.
(139)
It follows that
∥F(z)
1
2 PR(z)v −F(z)
1
2 Pf(z)v∥2 =∥K(z)F(z)
1
2 v −Pf(z)F(z)
1
2 v∥2
≤∥∆J∥2
λHF ,s
∥F(z)
1
2 v∥2.
(140)
Note that
∥∆Jq∥2 = ∥∇F †
s (w)[F(z)−1
2 q]⊤∇f(z)∥F (z)
(78)
≤δFs∥q∥2.
(141)
Substituting it into (140) yields
∥PR(z)v −Pf(z)v∥F (z) = ∥F(z)
1
2 PR(z)v −F(z)
1
2 Pf(z)v∥2 ≤
δFs
λHF ,s
∥v∥F (z).
(142)

Lemma J.9. Suppose Assumptions 7.1 and 7.2 hold. For any x, y ∈U and v ∈Rp, we have
∥F †

(145)
Proof. By Assumption 7.2,
∥F †

(78)

G κ
1

−1
2
F ∥x −y∥2∥v∥2
(146)
We can similarly prove (144) by (81). On the other hand, using ∇Pf + ∇Ps = 0, we get
∥Pf(x)v −Pf(y)v∥2 = ∥
Z 1
0

(147)
Lemma J.10. Suppose Assumptions 7.1 and 7.2 hold. The dynamics of zt = Φ(wt) satisfies
(
˙zt = −ηtχPR(zt)F(zt)−1(st + β2∇f(zt)) + ηtϵz,t,
˙st = −αst + ∇f(zt),
(148)
where s0 = 0 and
∥ϵz,t∥2 ≲
Z t
0

J.3. Useful Lemmas
Lemma J.13 (Cauchy’s Inequality). For any vector u, v and positive semi-definite matrices A and F, by using u⊤Av ≤
∥A
1
2 u∥2∥A
1
2 v∥2 ≤∥A∥2∥u∥2∥v∥2, we have
u⊤Av = u⊤F
1
2 F −1
2 AF −1
2 F
1
2 v ≤∥F −1
2 AF −1
2 ∥2∥u∥2∥v∥2.
(183)
Lemma J.14. Let at, bt ≥0 be real-valued functions defined for t ≥0. Suppose at is continuously differentiable and bt is
continuous with respect to t. If there exists a constant λ > 0 such that
d
dtat ≤−λat + √at
p
bt,
(184)
then for all t ≥0,
√at ≤e−λ
2 t√a0 + 1
2
Z t
0
e−λ
2 (t−s)p
bsds.
(185)
42


--- Page 43 ---
Accelerating LLM Pre-Training through Flat-Direction Dynamics Enhancement
Proof. Let δ > 0 be an arbitrary small constant. Define a perturbed variable yt as yt = √at + δ. Since at ≥0, we have
yt ≥
√
δ > 0 for all t. Thus, yt is continuously differentiable. Substituting d
dtyt into (184) gives
d
dtyt ≤−λ
2 yt + λδ
2yt
+
√bt
2
p
y2

2
.
(186)
It follows that
d



2
+ λ

δ
2
!
.
(187)

yt ≤e−λ
2 ty0 + 1

Z t
0
e−λ
2 (t−s)p
bs ds + λ
√
δ

Z t
0
e−λ

(188)
Take the limit as δ →0, we get the conclusion.
Remark J.15. It seems natural to divide the inequality (184) by √at and consider d
dt
√at. However, direct differentiation of
√at is not well-defined when at = 0. To handle this singularity strictly, we introduce a regularization term δ.
Lemma J.16 (Gronwall’s Inequality). Let ut, αt and βt ≥0 be continuous functions for t ≥0. If
ut ≤αt +
Z t
0
βsusds,
(189)
then
ut ≤αt +
Z t
0
αsβs exp
Z t
s
βτdτ

ds.
(190)
Additionally, of αt is non-decreasing, then
ut ≤αt exp
Z t
0
βτdτ

.
(191)
Lemma J.17 (Wedin sin(Θ) Theorem). Let M and N be two matrices with singular values σ1, . . . , σn and ˜σ1, . . . , ˜σn,
respectively. Denote by UM, VM the first k left and right singular vectors of M, and by UN, VN those of N. Suppose there
exists α > 0 such that
α ≤min
1≤i≤k σi −
max
k+1≤r≤n ˜σr.
Then
max

∥UM −UN∥2, ∥VM −VN∥2
	
≤
M −N

2
α
.
43
